title: Construction equipment operators
exposure_rationale: Construction equipment operators perform highly physical, hands-on work in unpredictable outdoor environments, requiring real-time manual control of heavy machinery. While AI and automation (e.g., GPS-guided grading, semi-autonomous equipment) may assist with precision tasks, the core job demands physical presence, situational awareness, and manual dexterity that remain difficult to automate at scale. The occupation may see incremental productivity improvements from technology but is unlikely to face major workforce restructuring from AI in the near term.
education_detail: A high school diploma or equivalent is typically required to become a construction equipment operator. Vocational training and math courses are useful, and a course in automotive mechanics may be helpful because workers often maintain their equipment. Learning at vocational schools may be beneficial in finding a job. Schools may specialize in a particular brand or type of construction equipment. Some schools incorporate sophisticated simulator training into their courses, allowing beginners to familiarize themselves with the equipment in a virtual environment before operating real machines.
certifications: Construction equipment operators often need a commercial driver’s license (CDL) to haul their equipment to various jobsites. State laws governing CDLs vary. A few states have special licenses for operators of backhoes, loaders, and bulldozers. Some states and cities require pile driver operators to have a crane license, because similar operational concerns apply to both pile drivers and cranes. Requirements vary by state. For more information, contact your local or state licensing board.

title: Construction laborers and helpers
exposure_rationale: Construction laborers and helpers perform almost entirely physical, hands-on work — digging, lifting, operating tools, cleaning sites, and assisting tradespeople — that requires physical presence in unpredictable outdoor environments. AI has essentially no direct impact on these core tasks, though it might marginally assist with scheduling or site planning at the supervisory level. Robotics and automation pose a longer-term threat, but current construction robotics remain limited and expensive, and the occupation is actually projected to grow faster than average through 2034.
education_detail: Although laborers and helpers typically do not need a formal educational credential, employers may prefer to hire those who have a high school diploma or the equivalent. For example, an employer may require or prefer that helpers of electricians and helpers of pipelayers, plumbers, pipefitters, and steamfitters have completed high school. High school classes that may be helpful include mathematics, such as algebra and geometry, and those that are part of career and technical education programs, such as construction technology or welding.
certifications: Employers often require that construction laborers and helpers complete safety certification that meetsOccupational Safety and Health Administration(OSHA) guidelines, either before or after being hired. A valid driver’s license also may be required. Some workers choose to earn optional certifications related to their tasks, such as rigging or scaffolding. Certification helps workers demonstrate that they have the knowledge to perform more complex tasks.

title: Construction managers
exposure_rationale: Construction managers split their time between field-based oversight (visiting worksites, coordinating trades, responding to emergencies) and knowledge work (budgeting, scheduling, contract interpretation, reporting). AI can meaningfully assist with cost estimation, project scheduling, document analysis, and progress reporting, but the core of the job requires physical presence on job sites, real-time human judgment in unpredictable environments, and relationship management with subcontractors, inspectors, and clients. The strong projected job growth (9%) and the irreducibly physical nature of construction oversight limit how much AI can restructure this occupation.

ai_paths.reason: Deep familiarity with heavy machinery used on job sites.
Hands-on knowledge of mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems.

education_detail: Construction managers typically need a bachelor'sdegreeinconstruction,business,engineering, or a related field. Bachelor’s degree programs in construction-related majors often include courses in project control and management, design, construction methods and materials, and cost estimation. Courses in business, communications, and mathematics are also helpful. Some construction managers earn an associate’s degree in construction management or construction technology. An associate’s degree combined with work experience may be typical for managers who supervise small projects. Candidates who have a high school diploma and several years of relevant work experience may qualify to become construction managers. However, these people may be more likely to work as self-employed general contractors than to be hired as construction managers.
certifications: Some states require construction managers to be licensed. For more information, contact your state licensing board. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Professional certification, although not required, demonstrates a particular level of knowledge and experience. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Cooks
exposure_rationale: Cooking is fundamentally a physical, hands-on occupation requiring manual dexterity, sensory judgment (taste, smell), and real-time adaptation in a kitchen environment — all of which are highly resistant to AI automation. While AI might assist with recipe optimization, inventory management, or scheduling, the core work of preparing food cannot be done remotely or digitally. Some automation pressure exists for fast food cooks (already reflected in projected -13% employment decline), but restaurant and specialty cooks face minimal displacement from AI specifically.
education_detail: Cooks typically do not need formal education. However, employers may require or prefer that applicants have a high school diploma. Vocational cooking schools, professional culinary institutes, and some colleges offer programs and courses on topics such as cooking techniques and international cuisines. Programs generally last from a few months to 2 years, and applicants may be required to have a high school diploma or equivalent. Depending on the type and length of the program, graduates generally qualify for entry-level positions as a restaurant cook.

title: Correctional officers and bailiffs
exposure_rationale: Correctional officers and bailiffs perform fundamentally physical, presence-based work — maintaining order, conducting searches, restraining individuals, escorting people, and patrolling facilities — that cannot be done remotely or digitally. AI might assist with minor administrative tasks like report writing or log documentation, but the core job requires constant physical presence in unpredictable, high-stakes environments. The occupation's projected decline is driven by policy changes around incarceration, not AI displacement.
education_detail: Correctional officers and bailiffs typically must have at least a high school diploma or equivalent. For employment in federal prisons, theFederal Bureau of Prisonsrequires entry-level correctional officers to have a bachelor’s degree or several years of experience in a field providing counseling, assistance, or supervision. Bachelor's degreefieldsvary but commonly includesecurity and protective serviceor a related field, such associal science.
certifications: Some states require correctional officers to complete state certification. For more information, check with your state’s public safety, corrections, or other agency that establishes this certification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Cost estimators
exposure_rationale: Cost estimation is predominantly knowledge work involving data collection, analysis, and report generation — all tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. The BLS already notes that improving cost estimation software is reducing the number of workers needed, and AI can further automate blueprint reading, database comparisons, and cost modeling. However, the job retains meaningful human elements such as site visits, collaboration with engineers and clients, and judgment calls on project-specific variables that provide some buffer against full automation.
education_detail: Employers generally prefer to hire candidates who have a bachelor’s degree. Construction cost estimators typically need a bachelor'sdegreein a related field, such asconstructionorengineering. Manufacturing cost estimators typically need a degree inbusinessor finance.

title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
exposure_rationale: Court reporting and captioning are fundamentally transcription tasks — converting spoken audio to accurate text — which is precisely what AI speech-to-text systems are rapidly mastering. Automated transcription tools (e.g., Whisper, Otter.ai, Rev) already handle much of this work, and the BLS itself notes that 'technology may make transcription work more efficient' while projecting zero employment growth. The remaining human role is primarily quality review, legal certification, and handling edge cases (accents, crosstalk, technical terminology), but AI is advancing quickly in these areas too, making the occupation highly vulnerable to significant workforce reduction even if full automation isn't immediate.

ai_paths.reason: High-speed transcription and real-time communication skills transfer perfectly.
Expertise in audio equipment and live event recording.
Deep familiarity with legal proceedings and neutral documentation.
Strong attention to detail and legal evidence gathering.
Experience in meticulous record-keeping and historical documentation.
Knowledge of medical terminology and administrative accuracy.
Detailed reporting and understanding of regulatory compliance.

education_detail: Many court reporters and simultaneous captioners attend programs at community colleges or technical institutes that lead to either a certificate or an associate’s degree. Either credential qualifies applicants for many entry-level positions. Certification programs prepare students to pass the licensing exams and typing-speed tests required by most states and employers. Most court reporting programs include courses in English grammar and phonetics, legal procedures, and legal terminology. Students also practice preparing transcripts to improve the speed and accuracy of their work. Some schools also offer training in the use of different transcription equipment, such as stenotype machines or steno masks. Completing a court reporting program typically takes 2 or 3 years.
certifications: Many states require court reporters and simultaneous captioners to be licensed or certified by a professional association. Licensing requirements vary by state and by method of reporting or captioning. Certifications typically require the successful completion of a written test, as well as a skills test in which applicants must type, record, or transcribe a minimum number of words per minute with a high level of accuracy. In addition, professional associations often require court reporters and simultaneous captioners to obtain a certain amount of continuing education credits in order to renew their certification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. For more information on certification, exams, and continuing education requirements, visit the specific association’s website. State licensing and continuing education requirements are available on the state association’s or state judicial agency's website.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals
State government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Craft and fine artists
exposure_rationale: Craft and fine artists span a wide spectrum: physical craft work (pottery, glassblowing, sculpture, weaving) has very low AI exposure due to manual skill and material handling, while digital illustration, cartooning, and digital art face very high exposure from generative AI image tools. The occupation's value is also partly tied to human authenticity and provenance, which provides some protection, but AI-generated art is already displacing demand for illustrators and digital artists. The blended nature of the occupation — roughly half physical/craft and half digital/illustrative — lands it at moderate overall exposure, with significant variation by specialty.

ai_paths.reason: Uses artistic composition and color theory with botanical media.
Transfers sculptural techniques and material knowledge to furniture making.
Leverages expertise in color mixing, surfaces, and brush control.
Applies detailed painting and decorative design to a small-scale canvas.
Translates mosaic and pattern design skills to architectural surfaces.
Applies creative sculpting and decorative finishing to edible products.

education_detail: Most fine artists pursue postsecondary education to improve their skills and job prospects. A formal educational credential is typically not needed to be a craft artist. However, it is difficult to gain adequate artistic skills without some formal education. For example, high school art classes can teach prospective craft artists the basic drawing skills they need. A number of colleges and universities offer bachelor’s and master’s degrees infieldsrelated tofine and performing arts. In addition to studio art and art history, postsecondary programs may include core subjects, such as English, marketing, social science, and natural science. Independent schools of art and design also offer postsecondary education programs, which can lead to a certificate in an art-related specialty or to an associate’s, bachelor’s, or master’s degree in fine arts. Medical illustrators must have artistic ability and a detailed knowledge of human or animal anatomy, living organisms, and surgical and medical procedures. They usually need a bachelor’s degree that combines art and premedical courses. Medical illustrators may choose to get a master’s degree in medical illustration. Four accredited schools offer this degree in the United States. Education gives artists an opportunity to develop their portfolio, which is a collection of an artist’s work that demonstrates his or her styles and abilities. Portfolios are essential, because art directors, clients, and others look at them when deciding whether to hire an artist or to buy the artist’s work. In addition to compiling a physical portfolio, many artists choose to create a portfolio online. Those who want to teach fine arts at public elementary or secondary schools usually must have a teaching certificate in addition to a bachelor’s degree. For more information on workers who teach art classes, see the profiles onkindergarten and elementary school teachers,middle school teachers,high school teachers,career and technical education teachers, andpostsecondary teachers.

title: Credit counselors
exposure_rationale: Credit counseling involves significant knowledge work — analyzing finances, creating debt management plans, preparing documents, and explaining options — all of which AI can increasingly assist with or automate. However, the interpersonal counseling component (building rapport with financially stressed clients, negotiating with creditors, providing emotional support) creates a meaningful human-presence barrier. The BLS itself notes that 'automation software makes these workers more efficient and may temper employment growth,' signaling recognized AI impact, but the client-facing counseling relationship limits full displacement.

ai_paths.reason: Applying client advocacy and financial resource navigation skills.
Managing budgets and overseeing administrative operations.
Coordinating complex projects and managing financial costs.
Educating individuals on navigating complex institutional systems.
Developing personalized goal-oriented plans for client improvement.
Teaching practical financial literacy and professional skills.

education_detail: Credit counselors typically need a bachelor’s degree, usually in afieldsuch asbusiness, finance, orpsychology. Candidates with a high school diploma may qualify for some positions, even if employers prefer to hire those who have a degree. Prospective credit counselors may begin preparing in high school by taking classes in subjects such as English, mathematics, and personal finance. After they are hired, credit counselors typically need several months of on-the-job training to attain competency. During this training, new counselors work with more experienced counselors to learn how to build a rapport with clients and how to perform other duties.
certifications: Employers may prefer or require certification for credit counselors. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Colleges, universities, and professional schools; private
Colleges, universities, and professional schools; state
General medical and surgical hospitals; private

title: Customer service representatives
exposure_rationale: Customer service work is predominantly digital and phone-based, involving information retrieval, order processing, complaint handling, and communication — all tasks where AI (chatbots, voice agents, LLMs) is already highly capable and rapidly improving. The BLS itself projects a 5% employment decline driven explicitly by automation and self-service technology, and this projection likely understates AI's medium-term impact as conversational AI becomes increasingly sophisticated. The main barrier to full automation is handling complex, emotionally sensitive, or highly nuanced interactions, but even these are being eroded by advancing AI capabilities.
education_detail: Customer service representatives typically need a high school diploma or equivalent to enter the occupation. However, some of these workers have postsecondary education that may include a bachelor's degree infieldssuch asbusiness,communications, andsocial science.
certifications: Customer service representatives who provide information about finance and insurance may need a state license. Although licensing requirements vary by state, they usually include passing an exam. Some employers and organizations provide training for these exams.

title: Dancers and choreographers
exposure_rationale: Dancing is fundamentally a physical, embodied art form requiring years of athletic training, live performance, and human presence — areas where AI has minimal direct impact. Choreography has a slightly higher knowledge/creative component where AI tools might assist with music selection or routine visualization, but the core creative and physical execution remains deeply human. The audience demand is specifically for human performance, making AI substitution of the actual work product essentially irrelevant in the near term.
education_detail: Many dancers begin training when they are young and continue to learn throughout their careers. Ballet dancers begin training the earliest, usually between the ages of 5 and 8 for girls and a few years later for boys. Their training becomes more serious as they enter their teens, and most ballet dancers begin their professional careers by the time they are 18. Leading professional dance companies sometimes have intensive summer training programs from which they might select candidates for admission to their regular full-time training programs. Modern dancers normally begin formal training while they are in high school. They attend afterschool dance programs and summer training programs to prepare for their career or for a college dance program. Some dancers and choreographers pursue postsecondary education. Many colleges and universities offer bachelor’s and/or master’s degrees in dance, typically through departments of theater or fine arts. Most programs include coursework in a variety of dance styles, including modern dance, jazz, ballet, and hip-hop. Most entrants into college dance programs have previous formal training. Some choreographers work as dance teachers. Teaching dance in a college, high school, or elementary school requires a college degree. Some dance studios and conservatories prefer instructors who have a degree; however, they may accept previous work in lieu of a degree.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private

title: Data scientists
exposure_rationale: Data science is almost entirely computer-based work — coding, statistical modeling, data cleaning, algorithm development, and visualization — all domains where AI is advancing rapidly and already providing substantial productivity gains. AI tools like GitHub Copilot, automated ML platforms, and LLM-based data analysis can automate significant portions of routine data wrangling, model building, and even insight generation. However, the occupation retains meaningful human judgment requirements in problem framing, stakeholder communication, domain expertise application, and interpreting results in business context, preventing a maximum score.

ai_paths.reason: Expertise in complex math and programming transfers to academia.
Optimization and data-driven decision making apply to supply chains.
Using statistical models to improve efficiency and eliminate waste.
Applying analytical skills to improve healthcare facility operational efficiency.
Meticulous data collection and evidence analysis for legal investigations.
Modeling environmental data to assess risks and develop solutions.
Managing complex projects using quantitative scheduling and resource allocation.
Analyzing patterns and causes of diseases using statistical methods.

education_detail: Data scientists typically need at least a bachelor’s degree, but some jobs require a master’s or doctoral degree. Commonfields of degreeincludemathematics, statistics,computer science,business, andengineering. Because data science involves the use of algorithms and statistical techniques, students need extensive study in mathematics and statistics. High school students interested in becoming data scientists should take classes in subjects such as linear algebra, calculus, and probability and statistics. At the college level, courses in computer science are important in addition to math and statistics. Students must learn data-oriented programming languages as well as statistical, database, and other software for presenting analyses.

industries.name: Computer systems design and related services
Management, scientific, and technical consulting services

title: Database administrators and architects
exposure_rationale: Database administration and architecture is almost entirely computer-based knowledge work involving SQL, system design, security planning, and performance monitoring — all domains where AI tools are rapidly improving and already providing meaningful assistance. The BLS data itself notes that DBA employment is projected to decline slightly as cloud computing allows fewer administrators to serve more companies, a productivity dynamic that AI will likely amplify further. However, the architect role requires significant organizational judgment, stakeholder communication, and complex system design decisions that still benefit from human expertise, keeping the score from reaching the very highest tier.

ai_paths.reason: Technical leadership and oversight of organizational data strategy.
Applying database logic to optimize complex supply chain flows.
Managing healthcare data systems and ensuring regulatory compliance.
Teaching database theory and information technology to students.
Using organizational skills to manage complex, multi-phase technical projects.
Applying meticulous data collection and analytical verification skills.

education_detail: Database administrators and architects typically need a bachelor’s degree incomputer and information technologyor a relatedfield, such asengineering; some DBAs studybusiness. Employers may prefer to hire applicants who have a master’s degree focusing on data or database management, typically either in computer science, information systems, or information technology. Database administrators and architects need an understanding of database languages, such as Structured Query Language, or SQL. DBAs will need to become familiar with whichever programming language their firm uses.
certifications: Certification is typically offered directly from software vendors or vendor-neutral certification providers. Employers may require their database administrators and architects to be certified in the products they use.

industries.name: Computer systems design and related services
Educational services; state, local, and private

title: Delivery truck drivers and driver/sales workers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical — driving, loading/unloading cargo, navigating streets, and interacting with customers in person — which creates a natural barrier to AI automation. AI can assist with route optimization and scheduling (already widely used), but the core tasks of physically transporting and delivering goods cannot be done remotely or digitally. Autonomous vehicle technology poses a longer-term threat, but current regulatory, technical, and infrastructure limitations mean meaningful displacement is still distant, and the BLS projects strong growth through 2034.
education_detail: Delivery truck drivers and driver/sales workers typically enter the occupation with a high school diploma or equivalent.
certifications: All delivery drivers need a driver’s license.

title: Dental and ophthalmic laboratory technicians and medical appliance technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is primarily hands-on manufacturing work involving physical manipulation of materials, precision instruments, and specialized equipment, which limits direct AI automation of core tasks. However, AI-adjacent technologies like 3D printing, CAD/CAM software, and automated manufacturing equipment are already displacing dental lab technicians specifically, and the BLS projects a 5% decline in that sub-specialty. The job involves some digital design work (CAD software, reading prescriptions) where AI can assist, but the physical fabrication, fitting, and quality inspection components create a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement.

ai_paths.reason: Direct transfer of dental terminology and patient appliance knowledge.
Transferable skills in high-heat metal joining and material fabrication.
Experience with specialized resins, polishing, and fine detail finishing.
Strong foundation in sterile environments and medical instrument handling.

education_detail: Dental and ophthalmic laboratory technicians and medical appliance technicians typically need at least a high school diploma or equivalent. High school students interested in becoming a dental or ophthalmic laboratory technician or medical appliance technician may benefit from taking classes in science, mathematics, and art. Employers may prefer to hire candidates who have a postsecondary certificate or associate’s degree. Some community colleges and technical schools offer programs in dental or ophthalmic laboratory technology in which students gain experience completing specific tasks, such as surfacing and finishing prescription eyewear. Community colleges and technical schools also may offer programs in medical appliance technology fields, such as orthotics and prosthetics technology. Most dental and ophthalmic laboratory technicians and medical appliance technicians learn their skills through on-the-job training. They may begin as helpers and learn more advanced skills as they gain experience. For example, dental laboratory technicians may start out making models from impressions and progress to designing and fabricating crowns and bridges.
certifications: Although optional, certification may demonstrate a level of competence and professionalism that makes candidates more attractive to employers. It also may increase opportunities for advancement. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Dental assistants
exposure_rationale: Dental assistants perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — sterilizing instruments, assisting during procedures, taking X-rays, and working directly in patients' mouths — that requires physical presence and manual dexterity. AI can assist with peripheral administrative tasks like scheduling and recordkeeping, but the core job functions are deeply physical and interpersonal. The occupation has natural barriers to AI automation similar to other clinical support roles.
education_detail: Some states require dental assistants to graduate from an accredited program and pass an exam. Most programs are offered by community colleges, although they also may be offered by vocational or technical schools. Many dental assisting programs take about 1 year to complete and lead to a certificate or diploma. Programs that last 2 years are less common and lead to an associate’s degree. Accredited programs include classroom and laboratory work. Students learn about teeth, gums, jaws, and other areas thatdentistswork on and the instruments that dentists use. These programs also include supervised practical experience. High school students interested in a career as a dental assistant should take courses in anatomy, biology, and chemistry.
certifications: States typically do not require licenses for entry-level dental assistants. Some states require dental assistants to be licensed, registered, or certified for entry or advancement. For example, states may require assistants to meet specific licensing requirements in order to work in radiography (x ray), infection control, or other specialties. For specific requirements, contact your state’s Board of Dental Examiners. States that allow assistants to perform expanded duties, such as coronal polishing, require that they be licensed, registered, or hold certifications. To earn certification, applicants must pass an exam. The educational requirements for certification are that dental assistants must either have graduated from an accredited program or have a high school diploma and complete the required amount of work experience. Applicants also must have current certification in CPR (cardiopulmonary resuscitation). Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Dental hygienists
exposure_rationale: Dental hygienists perform highly physical, hands-on work — cleaning teeth, applying sealants, taking X-rays, and using specialized tools in patients' mouths — that fundamentally cannot be automated by AI. While AI might assist with X-ray analysis, documentation, or treatment planning on the periphery, the core job requires manual dexterity, physical presence, and direct patient interaction. The occupation is projected to grow, reflecting strong demand for in-person preventive care that AI cannot replicate.
education_detail: Dental hygienists typically need an associate’s degree in dental hygiene; they may also get a bachelor’s degree. Master’s degree programs in dental hygiene are available but are relatively uncommon. A bachelor’s or master’s degree usually is required for research, teaching, or clinical practice in public or school health programs. Dental hygiene programs are often found in community colleges, technical schools, and universities. Programs typically take 3 years to complete and offer laboratory, clinical, and classroom instruction. Areas of study include anatomy, medical ethics, and periodontics, which is the study of gum disease. High school students interested in becoming dental hygienists should take courses in biology, chemistry, and math. Most dental hygiene programs also require applicants to complete prerequisites, which often include college-level courses. Specific requirements vary by school.
certifications: Every state requires dental hygienists to be licensed; requirements vary by state. In most states, a degree from an accredited dental hygiene program and passing written and clinical examinations are required for licensure. To maintain licensure, hygienists must complete continuing education requirements. For specific requirements, contact your state’s Board of Dental Examiners. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Many jobs also require cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) certification.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Dentists
exposure_rationale: Dentistry is fundamentally a hands-on, physical occupation requiring fine motor dexterity to perform procedures in patients' mouths, which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI can assist with diagnostic imaging analysis (reading X-rays), treatment planning, and administrative tasks, but the core work — drilling, filling, extracting, and surgically operating — requires physical presence and manual skill that AI cannot replicate. The knowledge-work components (diagnosis, record-keeping, patient communication) are modest relative to the procedural demands, keeping overall AI exposure low.
education_detail: Dentists typically need a DDS or DMD degree from an accredited dental program. Most programs require that applicants have at least a bachelor’s degree and have completed certain science courses, such as biology or chemistry. Although no specific undergraduate major is required, programs may prefer applicants who have a bachelor'sdegreein a science, such asbiology. Applicants to dental schools usually take an entrance exam. Dental schools use this test along with other factors, such as grade point average, interviews, and recommendations, to admit students into their programs. Dental school programs typically include coursework in subjects such as local anesthesia, anatomy, periodontics (the study of oral disease and health), and radiology. All programs at dental schools include clinical experience in which students work directly with patients under the supervision of a licensed dentist. As early as high school, students interested in becoming dentists can take courses in subjects such as biology, chemistry, and math.
certifications: Dentists must be licensed in the state in which they work. All states require dentists to be licensed; requirements vary by state. Most states require a dentist to have a DDS or DMD degree from an accredited dental program, pass the written exam, and pass a state or regional clinical examination. In addition, a dentist who wants to practice in a dental specialty must have a license in that specialty. Licensure requires the completion of a residency after dental school and, in some cases, the completion of a special state exam. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Desktop publishers
exposure_rationale: Desktop publishing is almost entirely computer-based work involving layout design, text formatting, and graphic integration — all tasks that AI tools (like Adobe Firefly, Canva AI, and generative design features) are rapidly automating. The occupation is already in steep decline (-12% projected) as AI and improved software allow writers, editors, and designers to handle layout tasks themselves without specialists. The work product is fully digital with no physical component, placing it squarely in the very high exposure category.

ai_paths.reason: Transitioning from print production to digital media technical operations.
Applying visual composition and digital image editing expertise.
Creative application of color theory and aesthetic arrangement.
Managing information organization and document archival systems.
Scaling 2D layout skills into 3D environmental spaces.

education_detail: Desktop publishers usually need an associate’s degree, often in graphic design or graphic communications. Community colleges and technical schools offer desktop-publishing courses, which teach students how to create electronic page layouts and format text and graphics with the use of desktop-publishing software.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private
Civic, social, professional, and similar organizations

title: Diagnostic medical sonographers
exposure_rationale: Diagnostic medical sonographers perform hands-on physical work requiring direct patient interaction, manual transducer operation, and real-time image acquisition in clinical settings — all of which create significant barriers to AI automation. However, AI is increasingly capable of analyzing ultrasound images, detecting abnormalities, and assisting with interpretation, which could reduce the cognitive/analytical portion of the role. The core job remains physically grounded and patient-facing, but AI tools will meaningfully augment the image review and diagnostic summary components.
education_detail: High school students who are interested in becoming a diagnostic medical sonographer may benefit from taking classes in anatomy, physiology, physics, and math. Two- and 4-year colleges and universities offer associate’s and bachelor’sdegreeprograms in sonography. Certificate programs also are available from colleges and some hospitals. Sonography programs usually include courses in anatomy, medical terminology, and applied sciences, as well as specialized instruction in areas such as abdominal or breast sonography. Programs also typically include a clinical component in which students earn credit while working under the direction of an experienced worker in a hospital, a physician’s office, or an imaging laboratory.
certifications: Some diagnostic medical sonographers need a state-issued license. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Employers may require or prefer that these workers earn professional certification, which helps to show competency in the field. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. These workers also may need basic life support (BLS) or cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) certification.

title: Diesel service technicians and mechanics
exposure_rationale: Diesel mechanics perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — inspecting, repairing, and replacing mechanical components — that requires physical presence, dexterity, and real-world troubleshooting in unpredictable conditions. AI can assist with diagnostics (interpreting sensor data, identifying fault codes) and documentation, but cannot replace the manual labor of disassembling engines, replacing parts, or test-driving vehicles. The increasing use of electronic diagnostic tools represents incremental digitization, but the core job remains firmly physical and resistant to AI automation.
education_detail: Most employers require a high school diploma or equivalent. High school or postsecondary courses in automotive repair, electronics, and mathematics provide a strong educational background for a career as a diesel technician. Some employers prefer to hire workers with postsecondary education in diesel engine repair. Many community colleges and trade and vocational schools offer certificate or degree programs in diesel engine repair. These degree programs mix classroom instruction with hands-on training and include learning the basics of diesel technology, repair techniques and equipment, and practical exercises. Students also learn how to interpret technical manuals and electronic diagnostic reports.
certifications: Although not required, this certification demonstrates a diesel technician’s competence and experience to potential employers and clients, and often brings higher pay. Diesel technicians may be certified in specific repair areas, such as drivetrains, electronic systems, and preventative maintenance and inspection. To earn certification, technicians must have 2 years of work experience and pass one or more exams. To remain certified, diesel technicians must pass a recertification exam every 5 years. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Many diesel technicians are required to have a commercial driver’s license so that they may test-drive buses and large trucks.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Dietitians and nutritionists
exposure_rationale: Dietitians and nutritionists blend knowledge work (meal planning, interpreting research, creating educational materials, documentation) with significant interpersonal and clinical components (counseling clients, assessing health needs, coordinating with healthcare teams) that require human empathy and judgment. AI can meaningfully assist with generating meal plans, analyzing dietary data, and synthesizing nutrition research, potentially making individual practitioners more productive. However, the therapeutic relationship, motivational counseling, and physical assessment aspects create a meaningful barrier to full AI substitution, placing this occupation in the moderate-to-high exposure range.
education_detail: Dietitians and nutritionists typically need a bachelor's or master's degree in dietetics, food and nutrition, or a related field to enter the occupation. A graduate degree is required for some credentials. Graduate degree programs usually require applicants to have a bachelor'sdegreethat includes coursework in sciences such as anatomy, biology, and physiology.
certifications: Many states require dietitians and nutritionists to be licensed in order to practice. Other states require only state registration or certification to use certain titles, and a few states have no regulations for this occupation. Check with your state board for specific requirements. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Employers may prefer to hire candidates who have a professional credential, such as the Registered Dietitian (RD)/Registered Dietitian Nutritionist (RDN) or the Certified Nutrition Specialist (CNS) designation. Although these credentials are not always required, the qualifications may be the same as those necessary for becoming a licensed dietitian or nutritionist in states that require a license. The RD/RDN designation requires completion of a minimum of a master’s degree and a Dietetic Internship (DI), which includes supervised experience. Students may complete both criteria at once through a coordinated program, or they may finish their degree before applying for an internship. In order to maintain the RDN credential, dietitians and nutritionists must complete continuing professional education credits within a designated number of years. Many states accept the Certified Nutrition Specialist (CNS) credential or exam for licensure purposes. To qualify for the credential, applicants must have a master’s or doctoral degree, complete supervised experience, and pass an exam. To maintain the CNS credential, nutritionists must complete continuing education credits within a designated number of years. Dietitians and nutritionists may seek additional certifications in an area of specialty, such as diabetes education, oncology nutrition, or sports dietetics.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Drafters
exposure_rationale: Drafting is almost entirely computer-based work — converting designs into digital technical drawings using CAD and BIM software — making it highly susceptible to AI automation. AI tools are already capable of generating and refining technical drawings, interpreting specifications, and automating much of the conversion process that drafters perform. The BLS itself notes that CAD/BIM technologies are already reducing drafter employment by enabling engineers and architects to handle tasks previously done by drafters, and AI will accelerate this trend significantly.
education_detail: Drafters typically need an associate of applied science in drafting or a related degree from a community college or technical school. Some drafters prepare for the occupation by earning a certificate or diploma. Programs in drafting may include instruction in design fundamentals, sketching, and computer-aided design (CAD) software. It generally takes about 2 years of full-time education to earn an associate’s degree. Certificate and diploma programs vary in length but usually may be completed in less time. Students frequently specialize in a particular type of drafting, such as mechanical or architectural drafting. High school students may begin preparing by taking classes in mathematics, science, computer technology, design, computer graphics, and, where available, drafting.
certifications: Although not mandatory, certification demonstrates competence and knowledge of nationally recognized practices. Certifications are offered for several specialties, including architectural, civil, and mechanical drafting. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Drywall installers, ceiling tile installers, and tapers
exposure_rationale: Drywall installation and taping is almost entirely physical, hands-on work requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and on-site presence in construction environments. AI has essentially no ability to measure, cut, hang, or finish drywall panels. The only peripheral tasks where AI might assist are estimating material quantities or scheduling, but these represent a tiny fraction of the job.
education_detail: There are no educational credential requirements for becoming a drywall installer, ceiling tile installer, or taper, although some employers prefer to hire candidates who have a high school diploma or equivalent. A high school diploma or equivalent is typically required for workers starting an apprenticeship.

title: Economists
exposure_rationale: Economics work is almost entirely knowledge-based — research, data analysis, modeling, forecasting, and report writing — all domains where AI is rapidly advancing. AI tools can already assist significantly with data analysis, statistical modeling, literature review, and drafting reports, making individual economists substantially more productive. However, the occupation retains meaningful human judgment components: advising policymakers, interpreting novel economic phenomena, designing research frameworks, and communicating complex findings to diverse audiences, which tempers the score below the very highest tier.

ai_paths.reason: Applying economic data to community development and land use.
Expert knowledge transfer to students in a classroom setting.
Applying market trend analysis to determine property valuations.
Using logical reasoning and economic principles to resolve disputes.
Analyzing the economic impact of environmental policies and regulations.
Directing organizational strategy based on broad market and fiscal analysis.

education_detail: Economists typically need a master’s degree. Positions in business, research, or international organizations may require a master’s degree or Ph.D. and work experience. To pursue an advanced degree in economics, program applicants may need to have completed undergraduate coursework in subjects such as economics or mathematics. Candidates who have a bachelor’sdegreewith sufficient course credits in economics, statistics, or mathematics may qualify for some entry-level economist positions, including jobs with the federal government. Courses that introduce students to statistical analysis software also may be helpful. An advanced degree is sometimes required or preferred for higher level positions.

industries.name: State government, excluding education and hospitals
Management, scientific, and technical consulting services
Local government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Editors
exposure_rationale: Editing is almost entirely digital knowledge work — reading, rewriting, proofreading, fact-checking, and evaluating written content — all tasks where AI is already highly capable and rapidly improving. AI tools like large language models can already handle grammar correction, style suggestions, and even substantive rewrites, meaning editors using AI can be dramatically more productive (or fewer editors may be needed). The human judgment elements — selecting stories, guiding writers, making editorial policy decisions — provide some buffer, but the core mechanical and even many creative tasks are highly automatable, consistent with the occupation's already-stagnant job outlook.

ai_paths.reason: Transferable skills in storytelling, project oversight, and content evaluation.
Applying curriculum review skills and educational content development expertise.
Utilizing organizational skills for managing and verifying historical records.
Translating complex information into clear, instructional written materials.
Leveraging critical thinking and objective analysis of written evidence.
Sharing subject matter expertise through lectures and grading assignments.
Applying language arts mastery to student instruction and assessment.
Pivoting from text editing to technical media production workflows.

education_detail: Editors typically need a bachelor'sdegreeinEnglishor a related field, such ascommunicationsor journalism. Candidates with other backgrounds who can show strong writing skills also may find jobs as editors. Editors who deal with specific subject matter may need related work experience. For example, fashion editors may need expertise in fashion that they gain through formal training or work experience.

industries.name: Media streaming distribution services, social networks, and other media networks and content providers

title: Electrical and electronic engineering technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation blends significant hands-on physical work (assembling prototypes, calibrating equipment, soldering, physical testing, site visits) with knowledge-based tasks (writing reports, analyzing test data, drafting specifications) that AI can assist with. AI tools can increasingly help with design analysis, report writing, and simulation, but the core physical tasks of building, testing, and repairing hardware in labs and factories create a meaningful barrier to full AI displacement. The BLS already notes that automation of manual tasks is limiting growth, suggesting moderate but not transformative AI exposure.
education_detail: Associate’s degree programs in electrical or electronic engineering technology are available at community colleges and vocational–technical schools. Programs accredited by professional organizations typically include courses such as algebra, programming languages, physics, and circuitry. Depending on the job tasks or the industry, employers may prefer to hire candidates who have a bachelor’s degree. Candidates for other jobs may qualify with a high school diploma.
certifications: Certifications in a variety of fields are available for electrical and electronic engineering technologists and technicians. While optional, these credentials show that the designee has advanced knowledge. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Semiconductor and other electronic component manufacturing
Navigational, measuring, electromedical, and control instruments manufacturing

title: Electrical and electronics engineers
exposure_rationale: Electrical and electronics engineers do substantial knowledge work — design, simulation, documentation, analysis — that is increasingly assisted by AI tools (e.g., AI-aided circuit design, simulation optimization, code generation for embedded systems). However, a meaningful portion of the job involves physical inspection, lab testing, site visits, hands-on troubleshooting, and supervising manufacturing, which creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement. AI is likely to make individual engineers significantly more productive (accelerating design iteration, schematic generation, and documentation) but the deep domain expertise, physical validation requirements, and safety-critical judgment needed mean the occupation faces restructuring rather than elimination.
education_detail: High school students interested in studying electrical or electronics engineering benefit from taking courses in physics and math, including algebra, trigonometry, and calculus. Other courses, such as those in engineering, digital electronics, and drafting and design may also be helpful. Electrical and electronics engineers typically need a bachelor'sdegreein electrical engineering, electronics engineering, or a relatedengineeringfield. Programs include classroom, laboratory, and field studies. Courses include digital systems design, calculus, and electrical circuit theory. Students also may choose to participate in cooperative education programs or internships. Through partnerships with local businesses, these programs allow students to gain practical experience while they complete their education. Some employers may prefer to hire workers with a master’s degree.
certifications: Licensure is not required for entry-level electrical and electronics engineer positions. Experienced engineers may obtain a Professional Engineering (PE) license, which allows them to oversee the work of other engineers, sign off on projects, and provide services directly to the public. State licensure typically requires a bachelor’s or higher degree in engineering, a passing score on the Fundamentals of Engineering (FE) exam, several years of relevant work experience, and a passing score on the PE exam. Each state issues its own licenses. Most states recognize licensure from other states, as long as the licensing state’s requirements meet or exceed their own licensure requirements. Several states require continuing education for engineers to keep their licenses. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Electrical and electronics installers and repairers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally hands-on and physical — workers must physically inspect, disassemble, repair, and test electrical/electronic equipment in factories, substations, vehicles, and job sites. AI can assist with diagnostics, fault pattern recognition, and documentation, but cannot replace the physical manipulation, on-site judgment, and manual dexterity required. The core work product is a repaired physical system, not a digital artifact, which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation.
education_detail: Electrical and electronics installers and repairers must understand electrical equipment and electronics. As a result, employers often prefer applicants who have taken courses in electronics at a community college or technical school. Courses usually cover AC and DC electronics, electronic devices, and microcontrollers. It is important for prospects to choose schools that include hands-on training in order to gain practical experience.
certifications: While certification is not required, a number of organizations offer it, and it can be useful in getting a job. To become certified, applicants must meet prerequisites and pass a comprehensive exam. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Electrical power-line installers and repairers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and field-based, requiring workers to climb poles and towers, string cables, operate heavy equipment, and work in hazardous outdoor environments — tasks AI cannot perform. AI might assist with outage diagnostics, route optimization, or monitoring systems, but the core work of physically installing and repairing power lines remains firmly in the human domain. The strong projected job growth further reflects that this work cannot be meaningfully automated by AI.
education_detail: Electrical line installers and repairers typically need a high school diploma or equivalent. Employers may prefer to hire candidates who have basic knowledge of algebra and trigonometry. In addition, technical knowledge of electricity or electronics obtained through military service, vocational programs, or community colleges may be helpful. Some community colleges offer programs for electrical power-line installers and repairers that lead to a 1-year certificate or 2-year associate’s degree. These programs cover topics such as electrical distribution, line construction, and pole top and bucket rescue. The programs also may include an internship or hands-on fieldwork.
certifications: Although not mandatory, certification is available for electrical power-line installers and repairers. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Workers who drive heavy vehicles usually need a state-issued commercial driver’s license (CDL). Workers who drive crews that cross state lines need an interstate CDL, which may have additional requirements regulated by theFederal Motor Carrier Safety Administration. Other requirements, such as medical or other certifications and minimum age, vary by state. Check with your state for more details. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Electricians
exposure_rationale: Electricians perform highly physical, hands-on work that requires on-site presence, manual dexterity, and real-time problem-solving in unpredictable environments. AI might assist with blueprint reading, code lookups, or scheduling, but the core tasks—running wire, installing systems, troubleshooting physical faults—cannot be automated by AI. Strong job growth projections further confirm this occupation is insulated from AI displacement.
education_detail: A high school diploma or equivalent is required to become an electrician. Some electricians start out by attending a technical school. Many technical schools offer programs related to circuitry, safety practices, and basic electrical information. Graduates of these programs usually receive credit toward their apprenticeship.
certifications: Most states require electricians to pass a test and be licensed. Requirements vary by state. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. The tests have questions related to the National Electrical Code and state and local electrical codes, all of which set standards for the safe installation of electrical wiring and equipment. Electricians may be required to take continuing education courses in order to maintain their licenses. These courses are usually related to safety practices, changes to the electrical code, and training from manufacturers in specific products. Electricians may obtain additional certifications, which demonstrate competency in areas such as solar photovoltaic, electrical generating, or lighting systems. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Electricians may be required to have a driver’s license.

title: Electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is predominantly hands-on and physical, involving operating machinery, soldering, calibrating equipment, repairing hydraulic/pneumatic systems, and working at industrial sites — tasks that AI cannot directly perform. AI may assist with documentation, test result analysis, and troubleshooting diagnostics, but the core work requires physical dexterity, on-site presence, and manual skill. The BLS itself notes automation has mixed effects, still requiring humans to operate and maintain robotic equipment.

ai_paths.reason: Transfers expertise in electrical circuitry, wiring, and system diagnostics.
Combines complex mechanical systems with electronic control logic.
Utilizes mechatronic maintenance skills on large-scale renewable energy hardware.
Applies electrical system integration and technical troubleshooting skills.
Uses understanding of sensors, motors, and electronic controllers.

education_detail: Associate’s degree programs and postsecondary certificates for electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians are offered at vocational–technical schools and community colleges. Employers may prefer to hire graduates of programs accredited by professional organizations. Associate’s degree programs usually include courses in subjects such as algebra, trigonometry, and sciences. Depending on the program, students may have the option of concentrating in a field such as electromechanics, mechatronics, or industrial maintenance.
certifications: Electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians may earn optional certification to demonstrate professional competence. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Elementary, middle, and high school principals
exposure_rationale: School principals do substantial knowledge work (data analysis, reporting, scheduling, budgeting) that AI can assist with, but the core of the role is deeply interpersonal — managing staff, counseling students, engaging parents, and maintaining school culture — requiring physical presence and human judgment in unpredictable environments. AI tools may improve efficiency on administrative tasks but cannot replace the leadership, relationship-building, and real-time decision-making that define the job. The role's authority and accountability are inherently human-centered, limiting how much AI can restructure the occupation.

ai_paths.reason: Utilizes master's level education to support individual developmental goals.
Connects student engagement experience with therapeutic program development.

education_detail: Principals typically need a master’s degree in education leadership or education administration. These master’s degree programs teach prospective principals how to manage staff, create budgets, set goals, and work with parents and the community. To enter a master's degree program, candidates typically need a bachelor'sdegreeineducation, counseling, or a related field.
certifications: Most states require public school principals to be licensed as school administrators. Licensure requirements vary by state, but most require a master’s degree. Some states have alternative programs for candidates who do not have a master’s degree in education administration or leadership. Most states also require candidates to pass an exam and a background check. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Principals in private schools are not required to have a state-issued license.

title: Elevator and escalator installers and repairers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and hands-on, requiring workers to install, maintain, and repair complex mechanical and electrical systems in buildings. While AI might assist with diagnostics, troubleshooting guidance, or maintenance record-keeping, the core work — working in elevator shafts, connecting wiring, replacing mechanical components, and performing physical inspections — cannot be automated by AI. The job requires physical presence in unpredictable environments, manual dexterity, and real-time problem-solving with heavy equipment.
education_detail: A high school diploma or equivalent is typically required. High school classes in math, mechanical drawing, and physics may be helpful.
certifications: Most states require elevator and escalator installers and repairers to be licensed. Check with your state for more information. Although not required, certification shows competence and proficiency in the field. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Employers may require elevator and escalator installers to have a driver’s license or reliable transportation to travel to jobsites.

title: Emergency management directors
exposure_rationale: Emergency management directors do substantial knowledge work — planning, analysis, writing reports, coordinating information — that AI can meaningfully assist with, but the core of the job involves real-time crisis leadership, interpersonal coordination across agencies, and on-the-ground decision-making under stress that requires human judgment and physical presence. AI tools could enhance planning, damage assessment, and information synthesis, but the irreducibly human elements of commanding emergency operations, building trust with community stakeholders, and making high-stakes decisions in chaotic environments limit how much AI can displace. The role's heavy reliance on experience, relationships, and situational authority makes full automation implausible.
education_detail: Emergency management directors typically need a bachelor’sdegreeinsecurity and protective service,business, or emergency management. Some directors working in the private sector in business continuity management may need a degree in computer science, information systems administration, or anothercomputer and information technology(IT) field. Small municipalities or local governments may hire applicants whose highest level of educational attainment is a high school diploma. However, these applicants usually must have extensive work experience in emergency management if they are to be hired.
certifications: Some states require directors to obtain certification within a certain timeframe after being hired in the position. Many agencies and states offer voluntary certification programs to help emergency management directors obtain additional skills. Some employers may prefer or even require a Certified Emergency Manager (CEM), Certified Business Continuity Professional (CBCP), or equivalent designation. Certification must be renewed after a specified number of years. Some organizations require candidates to complete certain continuing education courses prior to recertification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: EMTs and paramedics
exposure_rationale: EMT and paramedic work is fundamentally physical and requires real-time human presence in unpredictable, high-stakes environments — responding to emergencies, physically treating and transporting patients, and performing hands-on medical procedures. AI may assist peripherally with documentation, dispatch optimization, or diagnostic support tools, but cannot replace the core physical and interpersonal demands of the job. The unpredictable nature of emergency scenes and the irreplaceable need for human judgment and physical intervention create strong natural barriers to AI displacement.
education_detail: High school students interested in becoming EMTs or paramedics should take courses in life sciences and consider becoming certified in cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR). Some high schools offer EMT training through vocational or technical education programs. EMTs typically need to complete a postsecondary education program to enter the occupation. Program applicants usually need a high school diploma or equivalent and certification in CPR and basic life support (BLS). However, program entry requirements vary by state. Most EMT programs lead to a nondegree award. They vary in length but typically take less than 1 year for EMT-Basic certification and up to 2 years for EMT-Intermediate certification. Programs are offered by technical institutes, community colleges, universities, and facilities that specialize in emergency care training. Some instruction may take place in a hospital or ambulance setting. EMT-Basic programs cover topics such as assessing patients’ conditions, handling emergencies, and using field equipment. Programs that do not require BLS and CPR certification prior to admission include instruction that leads to those certifications as part of the program. EMT-Intermediate programs require additional instruction for advanced skills, such as using complex airway devices, intravenous fluids, and some medications. Paramedic postsecondary programs require applicants to have EMT-Intermediate certification. Most programs at community colleges typically lead to a nondegree award or an associate’s degree. Some programs are offered by 4-year universities and lead to a bachelor’s degree. Paramedic programs include courses such as anatomy and physiology, EKG interpretation, and maintaining airflow. They typically include supervised field experience.
certifications: All states require EMTs and paramedics to be licensed; requirements vary by state. Most states require candidates to be certified, but others require passage of an equivalent state exam. States also may have other requirements for licensing, such as a minimum age and passing a background check. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Employers typically require job candidates to have a driver’s license and may prefer that they have ambulance driver certification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Entertainment and recreation managers
exposure_rationale: Entertainment and recreation managers split their time between administrative/planning tasks (scheduling, budgeting, strategy) that AI can assist with and hands-on, people-facing work (engaging customers, supervising staff, inspecting facilities, managing live events) that requires physical presence and interpersonal judgment. AI tools can meaningfully help with scheduling, budget analysis, customer feedback analysis, and communications, but the core value of the role lies in leadership, relationship management, and real-time operational oversight in physical settings. The strong projected job growth and inherently social/physical nature of the work limit AI's ability to substantially reduce headcount.
education_detail: Entertainment and recreation managers have a variety of academic backgrounds, ranging from a high school diploma to a bachelor’s or higher degree. Requirements vary by organization and the specific work that managers do. For example, a recreation manager might need a bachelor’sdegreein park management,recreation and fitness, or leisure studies. An entertainment manager might need a degree in theater, music, or a related visual orperforming artsfield. Some college students participate in internships. Through internships, students gain practical experience in their field of study while completing their education.
certifications: Some states may require a license for certain types of entertainment and recreation managers. For more information, contact your state licensing board. Safety certifications may be required, but professional certification is usually optional. For example, some employers require entertainment and recreation managers to have first aid or CPR certification. Optional credentials may demonstrate a particular level of knowledge and experience. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Environmental engineering technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of field/lab work (collecting samples, operating equipment, inspecting sites, handling hazardous materials) and knowledge work (reviewing documents, maintaining records, analyzing data). AI can meaningfully assist with the data analysis, documentation, and compliance review portions, but the core physical tasks — fieldwork, sample collection, equipment operation, and on-site inspections — require human presence and cannot be automated by AI. The BLS itself notes that automated environmental testing may limit employment growth, suggesting moderate but not transformative AI/automation impact.
education_detail: Prospective environmental engineering technologists and technicians should take science and math courses in high school to prepare for postsecondary programs in engineering technology. Employers usually prefer to hire candidates who have completed accredited postsecondary programs. Although some candidates may be hired with a high school diploma and postsecondary coursework, environmental engineering technologists and technicians typically need an associate’s degree in environmental engineering technology or a related field to enter the occupation. Associate degree programs in environmental engineering technology are available in community colleges and vocational–technical schools. These programs generally include courses in chemistry, environmental assessment, hazardous-waste management, and mathematics. Some environmental engineering technologists and technicians enter the occupation with a bachelor’s degree.
certifications: Some states require environmental technologists and technicians to have permits or licenses to remove hazardous waste. Workers also may be required to haveOccupational Safety & Health Administration(OSHA) Hazardous Waste Operations and Emergency Response Standard (HAZWOPER) certification. HAZWOPER certification includes training in health hazards, personal protective equipment, site safety, recognizing and identifying hazards, and decontamination. Refresher training may be required to maintain certification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Environmental engineers
exposure_rationale: Environmental engineers do substantial knowledge work — report writing, data analysis, regulatory compliance, and design — that AI can meaningfully assist with, pushing this toward high exposure. However, a significant portion of the job involves site inspections, fieldwork, physical system design requiring professional licensure, and coordination with diverse stakeholders in real-world settings, which limits full AI displacement. AI tools can accelerate report generation, data analysis, and regulatory research, but the combination of physical presence requirements, professional judgment, and PE-licensed sign-off authority keeps this from reaching the very high exposure tier.

ai_paths.reason: Applies engineering principles to oversee large-scale infrastructure projects.
Direct application of water chemistry and treatment knowledge.
Transfers expertise in environmental regulations and hazard mitigation.
Leverages technical mapping and site assessment engineering skills.
Applies ecological protection and natural resource management expertise.
Directly utilizes environmental monitoring and data collection skills.
Practical application of waste management and safety protocols.

education_detail: High school students interested in becoming an environmental engineer should take classes in chemistry, biology, physics, and math, including algebra, trigonometry, and calculus. Entry-level environmental engineering jobs typically require a bachelor’sdegreeinengineering. Programs usually include courses in subjects such as construction systems, engineering mechanics, and geochemistry and involve academic instruction, laboratory study, and fieldwork. Some college and university programs offer cooperative education in which students gain practical experience while completing their studies. Students also may get relevant experience through internships or by volunteering in positions that focus on the environment. Bachelor’s degree programs usually take 4 years, but some colleges and universities have 5-year engineering programs that lead to both a bachelor’s and a master’s degree. Employers often prefer to hire graduates of accredited engineering programs. A degree from an accredited program is usually necessary for engineers to become licensed.
certifications: Licensure is not required for entry-level environmental engineers. Experienced engineers may obtain a Professional Engineer (PE) license, which allows them to oversee the work of other engineers, sign off on projects, and provide services directly to the public. State licensure generally requires a bachelor’s or higher degree from an accredited engineering program, a passing score on the Fundamentals of Engineering (FE) exam, several years of relevant work experience, and a passing score on the PE exam. Each state issues its own license. Most states recognize licensure from other states if the licensing state’s requirements meet or exceed their own licensure requirements. Several states require engineers to take continuing education to keep their licenses. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. After licensing, environmental engineers can earn board certification. This certification shows that an environmental engineer has expertise in one or more areas of specialization. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Some states require environmentalengineersto haveOccupational Safety & Health Administration(OSHA)Hazardous Waste Operations and Emergency Response Standard(HAZWOPER) certification. HAZWOPER certification includes training in health hazards, personal protective equipment, site safety, recognizing and identifying hazards, and decontamination. Refresher training may be required to maintain certification.

title: Environmental science and protection technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation combines significant fieldwork (collecting samples, conducting inspections, operating monitoring equipment outdoors) with knowledge-based tasks (data analysis, report writing, compliance verification). AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and reporting components, but the core fieldwork—physical sample collection, site inspections, and hands-on equipment operation—requires human presence and cannot be automated. The mix of physical/field duties and office/lab work places this squarely in the moderate exposure range.

ai_paths.reason: Expertise in handling environmental contaminants and safety protocols.
Transfers field data collection and natural resource monitoring.
Utilizes understanding of chemical applications and environmental impact.
Applies technical field skills to renewable energy projects.
Transfers laboratory specimen handling and data recording abilities.

education_detail: Environmental science and protection technicians typically need at least an associate’s degree in environmental science, environmental health, or public health, or a related field. Depending on the position, however, educational requirements may vary from a high school diploma to a bachelor’sdegree. A background in natural sciences—including classes in chemistry, biology, and physics—is important for environmental science and protection technicians. College students should take science courses that provide laboratory experience. Coursework in math, statistics, and computer science also is useful for data analysis and modeling.
certifications: Some states have specific requirements, such as a license or registration, for environmental science and protection technicians. Check with your state licensing agency for more information. Employers may prefer that workers have or plan to obtain a regular driver’s license or commercial driver’s license (CDL). OSHA certification, such as the HAZWOPER, also may be helpful. These workers also may obtain optional certification to conduct certain types of environmental and health inspections. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.
