The job outlook is actually growing faster than average, reflecting strong demand for human technicians despite increasing equipment computerization.
education_detail: Most heavy vehicle and mobile equipment service technicians have a high school diploma or equivalent. High school courses in automotive repair, electronics, physics, and welding provide a strong foundation for a service technician’s career. However, high school graduates often need further training to become fully qualified. Completing a vocational or other postsecondary training program in diesel technology or heavy equipment mechanics is increasingly considered the best preparation for some entry-level positions. Offered by vocational schools and community colleges, these programs cover the basics of diagnostic techniques, electronics, and other related subjects. Each program may last 1 to 2 years and lead to a certificate of completion. Other programs, which lead to associate’s degrees, generally take 2 years to complete.
certifications: Some manufacturers offer certification in specific repair methods or equipment. Although not required, certification can demonstrate a service technician’s competence and usually commands higher pay.

title: High school teachers
exposure_rationale: High school teaching involves a significant mix of physical presence, interpersonal relationship-building, and classroom management that AI cannot easily replicate, alongside knowledge-work tasks like lesson planning, grading, and content delivery where AI can provide substantial assistance. AI tools can already automate grading, generate lesson plans, personalize learning materials, and explain concepts, potentially making each teacher more productive or reducing demand at the margins. However, the core value of teaching — motivating students, managing classroom dynamics, mentoring, and adapting in real-time to human behavior — remains deeply human and requires physical presence, limiting how far AI can restructure the role.
education_detail: All states require public high school teachers to have at least a bachelor'sdegree, which may be ineducation. Many states require high school teachers to have majored in a content area, such asmathematicsor science. Teacher education programs typically provide instruction on presenting information to students of different abilities and backgrounds. Programs typically include a student-teaching program, in which prospective teachers work with a mentor teacher and get experience instructing students in a classroom. Some states require high school teachers to earn a master’s degree after earning their teaching certification and obtaining a job. Teachers in private schools do not need to meet state requirements. However, private schools typically seek high school teachers who have a bachelor’s degree and a major in a subject area.
certifications: All states require teachers in public schools to be licensed or certified in the specific grade level they will teach. Those who teach in private schools typically are not required to be licensed. High school teachers typically are awarded a secondary or high school certification, which allows them to teach the 7th through the 12th grades. Requirements for certification or licensure vary by state but generally involve the following: A bachelor’s degree with a minimum grade point averageCompletion of a student-teaching programPassing a background checkPassing a general teaching certification test, as well as a test that demonstrates their knowledge in the subject they will teach. Teachers often are required to complete professional development classes to keep their license or certification. Some states require teachers to complete a master’s degree after receiving their certification and obtaining a job. All states offer an alternative route to certification or licensure for people who already have a bachelor’s degree but lack the education courses required for certification. Some alternative certification programs allow candidates to begin teaching immediately with supervision by an experienced teacher. These programs cover teaching methods and other topics, such as resource management. After they complete the program, candidates are awarded full certification. Other programs require students to take classes in education before they can teach.

title: Historians
exposure_rationale: Historians do predominantly knowledge work — researching, analyzing, interpreting, and writing about historical sources — which is highly amenable to AI assistance in literature review, synthesis, translation, and drafting. However, the job also involves physical archival work, artifact handling, fieldwork, public engagement, and nuanced interpretive judgment that requires deep domain expertise and contextual understanding. AI can significantly accelerate research and writing tasks, but the scholarly judgment, source authentication, and public-facing roles retain meaningful human requirements.

ai_paths.reason: Applying deep historical knowledge to educational storytelling and site interpretation.
Utilizing project oversight and complex documentation management skills.
Applying research methodology to land preservation and resource management.
Using investigative research and evidence-based analysis to determine origins.
Leveraging analytical reporting and regulatory compliance research skills.
Translating strategic analysis and performance evaluation into team leadership.
Transferring instructional expertise and curriculum development to vocational training.

education_detail: Historians typically need a master’s degree or Ph.D. to enter the occupation. Many historians have a master’s degree in history or public history. Others complete degrees in related fields, such as museum studies, historical preservation, or archival management. In addition to coursework, most master’s programs in public history and similar fields require an internship as part of the curriculum. Research positions in the federal government and positions in academia typically require a Ph.D. Students in history Ph.D. programs usually concentrate in a specific area of history. Possible specializations include a particular country or region, period, or field, such as social, political, or cultural history. Candidates with a bachelor’sdegreeinhistorymay qualify for entry-level positions at museums, historical associations, or other small organizations. However, most bachelor’s degree holders usually work outside of traditional historian jobs—for example, jobs in education, communications, law, business, publishing, or journalism.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals
State government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Home health and personal care aides
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — bathing, dressing, lifting, cooking, and providing companionship to clients in their homes. AI cannot replicate the hands-on caregiving, physical presence, or emotional support that defines this role. AI might marginally assist with scheduling, record-keeping, or care plan documentation, but these are peripheral to the core job, which is projected to grow 17% as demand for in-person care rises.
education_detail: Home health and personal care aides typically need a high school diploma or equivalent, although some positions do not require a formal educational credential. Postsecondary nondegree award programs are available at community colleges and vocational schools.
certifications: Home health and personal care aides may need to meet requirements specific to the state in which they work. For example, some states require home health aides to have a license or certification, which may involve completing training and passing a background check and a competency exam. For more information, check with your state board of health. Certified home health or hospice agencies that receive payments from federally funded programs, such as Medicare, must comply with regulations regarding aides’ employment. Private care agencies that do not receive federal funds may have other employment requirements that vary by state. Aides also may be required to obtain certification in first aid and cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).

industries.name: Residential intellectual and developmental disability facilities
Continuing care retirement communities and assisted living facilities for the elderly

title: Human resources managers
exposure_rationale: HR managers do predominantly knowledge-based work — writing policies, analyzing workforce data, managing compliance, and communicating — all areas where AI can provide substantial assistance. However, the role is heavily interpersonal, involving dispute mediation, employee relations, executive consultation, and leadership of teams, which require human judgment and trust that AI cannot fully replicate. AI will likely automate routine HR tasks (payroll processing, benefits administration, initial screening) and boost individual productivity, but the strategic advisory and relationship-management core of the role provides meaningful insulation from full displacement.

ai_paths.reason: Managing personnel, safety compliance, and complex project scheduling.
Overseeing staff recruitment, training, and organizational operations.
Handling employee relations, hiring, and operational management.
Applying interpersonal skills and behavioral assessment to programs.
Leveraging expertise in workplace regulations and employee welfare.
Utilizing conflict resolution and benefits navigation experience.

education_detail: Human resources managers typically need a bachelor’s degree to enter the occupation. The degree may be in human resources or anotherfield, such asbusiness,communications, orpsychology. Courses in subjects such as conflict management may be helpful. Some jobs may require a master’s degree in human resources, labor relations, or business administration (MBA).
certifications: Although certification is voluntary, it shows professional expertise and credibility, and it may enhance job opportunities. Employers may prefer to hire candidates with certification, and some positions may require it. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Human resources specialists
exposure_rationale: HR specialists do predominantly knowledge-based, office work involving recruiting, screening, record-keeping, and compliance — all areas where AI tools are already making workers significantly more productive (e.g., resume screening, job description writing, background check automation, policy Q&A). However, a meaningful portion of the role involves interpersonal judgment — interviewing candidates, resolving employee disputes, building relationships — which still requires human presence and nuanced social skills. The net effect is high but not transformative exposure: AI will reshape workflows and reduce headcount needs at the margins, but the human-facing and judgment-intensive core of the role provides a meaningful buffer.

ai_paths.reason: Transfers project coordination, scheduling, and personnel management skills.
Applies expertise in staffing, regulatory compliance, and employee relations.
Uses interviewing, resource navigation, and interpersonal advocacy skills.
Leverages knowledge of workplace regulations and employee welfare.
Utilizes outreach, benefits navigation, and relationship-building expertise.
Transfers professional development, coaching, and workforce readiness knowledge.

education_detail: Human resources specialists typically need a bachelor'sdegreein human resources,business,communications, or a related field. By working in an internship during college, students gain relevant experience that may be helpful in competing for human resources specialist jobs. Internships in human resources departments may help prospective specialists to increase their understanding of the occupation and to network in an industry.
certifications: Professional associations that specialize in human resources offer courses to enhance the skills of their members, and some offer certification programs. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Certification usually requires that candidates pass an exam that covers human resources knowledge and asks candidates to apply their knowledge to different situations. Candidates for certification also typically need to meet minimum education and experience requirements. Although certification is usually voluntary, some employers prefer or require it. Human resources generalists, in particular, may benefit from certification because it shows knowledge and professional competence across all human resources areas.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Hydrologists
exposure_rationale: Hydrology involves significant knowledge work — data analysis, modeling, report writing, and forecasting — that AI can meaningfully assist with, particularly in processing large datasets, running predictive models, and automating routine analysis. However, a substantial portion of the job requires physical fieldwork: collecting water and soil samples, measuring streamflow, and traveling to remote locations, which AI cannot replace. The BLS itself notes that 'integrated technology and review systems may limit the need for some hydrologists,' suggesting AI-driven tools are already beginning to reshape the occupation, but the irreducible field component keeps exposure from reaching the highest tiers.
education_detail: Hydrologists typically need a bachelor’sdegreein aphysical sciencefield, such as geology or hydrology. Some employers prefer to hire candidates who have a master’s degree. Environmental science, geosciences, or other natural or physical science programs usually include coursework in math, statistics, and sciences. Students also may choose to participate in internships while in school to gain hands-on experience before entering the job market. States that require geologists to be licensed may encourage hydrologists to pursue licensing as well.

industries.name: State government, excluding education and hospitals
Management, scientific, and technical consulting services
Local government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Industrial designers
exposure_rationale: Industrial design is predominantly knowledge and creative work done on computers — sketching, CAD modeling, rendering, client communication — all domains where AI is rapidly advancing and already useful (generative design, AI-assisted CAD, image generation). However, the job retains meaningful physical and interpersonal components: creating physical prototypes, traveling to manufacturing sites, collaborating with engineers, and consulting with clients, which provide some natural barriers to full AI displacement. AI tools will likely make individual designers substantially more productive (faster ideation, automated rendering, generative design exploration), potentially reducing headcount needed, placing this firmly in the high-exposure range.
education_detail: A bachelor’s degree is usually required for entry-level industrial design jobs. Commonfields of degreeincludefine arts,engineering, orarchitecture. Most industrial design programs include courses in drawing, computer-aided design and drafting (CADD), and three-dimensional modeling, as well as courses in business, industrial materials and processes, and manufacturing methods. More than 360 postsecondary colleges, universities, and independent institutes are accredited with programs in art and design. Many schools require successful completion of some basic art and design courses before granting entry into a bachelor’s degree program. Applicants also may need to submit sketches and other examples of their artistic ability. Many programs provide students with the opportunity to build a professional portfolio of their designs from classroom projects, internships, or other experiences. Students can use these examples of their work to demonstrate their design skills when applying for jobs and bidding on contracts for work.

title: Industrial engineering technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: This role combines significant knowledge work (data analysis, workflow design, statistical studies, preparing charts and diagrams) with substantial physical presence requirements — technicians must work on-site at manufacturing facilities to observe operations, conduct time-motion studies, and assess machinery. AI can meaningfully assist with data collection analysis, process optimization modeling, and documentation, but the core observational and hands-on assessment tasks require physical presence in production environments. The role's slow projected growth and emphasis on automation assistance suggests AI will augment rather than eliminate these workers in the near term.
education_detail: High school students interested in becoming industrial engineering technologists and technicians should take courses in math, science, and drafting, where available. Postsecondary programs in industrial engineering technology are offered at vocational–technical schools, technical institutes, and community colleges. Vocational–technical schools typically award a certificate. Community colleges programs usually lead to associate’s degrees. Employers may prefer to hire candidates who have completed an accredited engineering or engineering technology program. Industrial engineering technologists and technicians may choose to major in applied science, industrial technology, or industrial engineering technology. These programs may include instruction in computer-aided design/computer-aided manufacturing software, known as CAD/CAM.

title: Industrial engineers
exposure_rationale: Industrial engineers do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, process optimization, system design, and reporting — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, making individual engineers significantly more productive. However, a meaningful portion of the job involves physical site visits, observing workers and processes firsthand, collaborating across departments, and implementing changes in real-world environments, which creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement. The occupation's projected growth suggests AI is more likely to augment these engineers (enabling fewer to do more) than to eliminate the role outright, but productivity gains could moderate headcount growth over time.
education_detail: High school students interested in industrial engineering should take classes in mathematics, such as algebra, trigonometry, and calculus; computer science; and sciences, such as chemistry and physics. Industrial engineers typically need a bachelor'sdegreein industrialengineeringor a related field. Some prospective industrial engineers have degrees in other engineering disciplines, such as mechanical, manufacturing, or general engineering. Human factors engineers may need a bachelor’s degree inpsychology. Bachelor’s degree programs include academic and laboratory coursework in subjects such as statistics, production systems planning, and manufacturing systems design. Colleges and universities may offer internship or cooperative education programs with businesses, allowing students to gain work experience while completing their education. Some colleges and universities offer 5-year programs that lead to both a bachelor’s and a master’s degree.
certifications: Licensure is not required for entry-level industrial engineer positions. Experienced engineers may obtain a Professional Engineer (PE) license, which allows them to oversee the work of other engineers, sign off on projects, and provide services directly to the public. State licensure typically requires a bachelor’s or higher degree in engineering, a passing score on the Fundamentals of Engineering (FE) exam, several years of relevant work experience, and a passing score on the PE exam. Each state issues its own license. Most states recognize licensure from other states, as long as the licensing state’s requirements meet or exceed their own licensure requirements. Several states require continuing education for engineers to keep their licenses. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Although certification is not required, some industrial engineers choose to earn a professional credential. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Industrial machinery mechanics, machinery maintenance workers, and millwrights
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally hands-on and physical — mechanics and millwrights must be physically present to disassemble, repair, calibrate, and install industrial machinery. AI may assist with predictive maintenance scheduling, diagnostic analysis, and reading technical manuals, but the core work of physically fixing and installing complex machinery cannot be automated by AI. The strong projected job growth (13%) driven by increased automation in manufacturing actually suggests complementarity with technology rather than displacement.
education_detail: Industrial machinery mechanics, machinery maintenance workers, and millwrights generally need at least a high school diploma or equivalent. Some mechanics and millwrights complete a 2-year associate’s degree program in industrial maintenance. Industrial maintenance programs may include courses such as welding, mathematics, hydraulics, and pneumatics.

title: Industrial production managers
exposure_rationale: Industrial production managers split their time between office-based knowledge work (analyzing production data, reviewing reports, budgeting, scheduling) and physical plant oversight requiring on-site presence, safety compliance, and real-time human leadership. AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and planning tasks — optimizing schedules, flagging quality issues, forecasting supply needs — but the core job involves managing people, navigating physical production environments, and making judgment calls that require contextual, on-the-ground awareness. The strong interpersonal, leadership, and problem-solving dimensions tied to physical operations create a natural ceiling on AI displacement.
education_detail: Employers typically require or prefer that industrial production managers have a bachelor’s degree. However, some workers qualify for jobs if they have a high school diploma and extensive production experience. For workers who have adegree, common majors includebusinessandengineering. Some employers prefer to hire industrial production managers who have a Master of Business Administration (MBA) or a graduate degree in industrial management.
certifications: Although they are not required to do so, industrial production managers may earn certifications to demonstrate competency in quality or management systems. Because these credentials often require specific work experience, they typically are not available prior to entering the occupation. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Information clerks
exposure_rationale: Information clerks perform largely routine, digital tasks — maintaining records, processing data, answering standard inquiries, and preparing reports — that are highly amenable to AI automation through chatbots, automated form processing, and intelligent document management systems. The BLS already projects employment decline driven by self-service systems and electronic data collection, confirming AI and automation are actively displacing these roles. Some sub-occupations (hotel desk clerks, eligibility interviewers) retain meaningful face-to-face human interaction that provides partial insulation, keeping the score from reaching the highest tier.

ai_paths.reason: Transfers information dissemination and guest service expertise to hospitality.
Combines administrative proficiency with basic clinical support tasks.
Uses organizational skills to help clients navigate complex public resources.
Leverages record-keeping and logistical accuracy in a physical environment.
Pivots information-sharing skills toward public health advocacy and outreach.
Translates customer service and safety communication to the aviation industry.

education_detail: Although candidates for most of these positions usually qualify with a high school diploma, human resources assistants generally need an associate’s degree. Regardless of whether they pursue a degree, courses in word processing and spreadsheet applications are particularly helpful.

title: Information security analysts
exposure_rationale: Information security analysis is predominantly knowledge work done on computers — monitoring networks, analyzing threats, writing reports, and developing security policies — making it highly amenable to AI augmentation. AI tools are already being deployed for threat detection, vulnerability scanning, and log analysis, meaning each analyst can handle more with AI assistance, potentially reducing headcount needs over time. However, the adversarial, rapidly evolving nature of cybersecurity requires continuous human judgment, creative problem-solving, and organizational communication that keeps this from reaching the highest exposure tier, and ironically AI-driven threats are simultaneously increasing demand for these workers.

ai_paths.reason: Leverages technical security expertise for high-level IT leadership.
Applies digital forensics and investigative logic to physical cases.
Transfers disaster recovery and risk mitigation planning skills.
Applies evidence collection and analytical methodology to investigations.
Utilizes project oversight, compliance, and complex systems management.
Shares specialized cybersecurity knowledge in a classroom setting.
Pivots from digital safety to physical system risk prevention.
Applies data privacy and regulatory compliance to healthcare.

education_detail: Information security analysts typically need a bachelor’sdegreeincomputer and information technologyor a related field, such asengineeringormath. However, some workers enter the occupation with a high school diploma and relevant industry training and certifications.
certifications: Many employers prefer to hire candidates who have information security certification. Some of these certifications are for workers at the entry level; others, are designed for experienced information security workers. Certification in specialized areas, such as systems auditing, also is available. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Computer systems design and related services
Management, scientific, and technical consulting services

title: Instructional coordinators
exposure_rationale: Instructional coordinators do substantial knowledge work — analyzing student data, developing curricula, reviewing educational materials, and writing training content — all of which AI can meaningfully assist with or accelerate. However, a significant portion of the role involves interpersonal and leadership activities: observing teachers in classrooms, conducting workshops, mentoring staff, and building relationships with administrators, which require physical presence and human judgment. AI tools could make individual coordinators substantially more productive (e.g., faster curriculum analysis, automated data review), potentially reducing headcount, but the relational and institutional dimensions of the job create a meaningful barrier to full automation.
education_detail: Instructional coordinators in public schools are required to have a master’s degree in education or curriculum and instruction. Some instructional coordinators need a degree in a specialized field, such as math or history. Master’s degree programs in curriculum and instruction teach about curriculum design, instructional theory, and collecting and analyzing data. To enter these programs, candidates usually need a bachelor’sdegreeineducation.
certifications: Instructional coordinators in public schools may be required to have a license, such as a teaching license or an education administrator license. For information about teaching licenses, see the profiles onkindergarten and elementary school teachers,middle school teachers, andhigh school teachers. For information about education administrator licenses, see the profile onelementary, middle, and high school principals. Check with your state’s Board of Education for specific license requirements.

industries.name: Colleges, universities, and professional schools; state
Colleges, universities, and professional schools; private
Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Insulation workers
exposure_rationale: Insulation work is fundamentally a hands-on, physical trade requiring workers to measure, cut, fit, and install materials in confined spaces, on scaffolding, and around complex mechanical systems — tasks that cannot be performed remotely or digitally. AI might marginally assist with blueprint analysis or material quantity estimation, but the core job involves physical dexterity, strength, and on-site judgment that AI cannot replicate. This occupation has very low AI exposure and is far more constrained by physical labor availability than by information processing.
education_detail: There are no specific education requirements for floor, ceiling, and wall insulators. Apprenticeships for mechanical insulators typically require a high school diploma or equivalent. High school courses in subjects such as math, mechanical drawing, and science are helpful for all types of insulators.
certifications: Insulation workers who remove and handle asbestos must be trained through programs accredited by theU.S. Environmental Protection Agency. Some states require a license for asbestos abatement. Check with your state for more information. Insulation workers may choose to get optional certification to demonstrate competency and to broaden employment opportunities. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Foundation, structure, and building exterior contractors

title: Insurance sales agents
exposure_rationale: Insurance sales is primarily a knowledge and communication-based role involving explaining policies, analyzing client needs, and maintaining relationships — all areas where AI can provide significant assistance through lead generation, policy comparison, document drafting, and customer interaction tools. However, the job retains a strong interpersonal and trust-building component that is difficult to fully automate, as clients often rely on human agents for guidance on complex, high-stakes financial decisions. AI is already reducing demand at the margins (online self-service purchasing), but the consultative, relationship-driven core of the role provides a meaningful buffer against full automation.

ai_paths.reason: Applies investigative techniques and high-stakes interpersonal communication.
Leverages experience explaining complex policies to diverse individuals.
Utilizes risk assessment and detailed policy compliance knowledge.
Applies analytical evaluation of property and liability risks.
Combines sensitive client consultation with contract administration.

education_detail: A high school diploma is typically required for insurance sales agents. However, some employers prefer to hire candidates who have a bachelor’s degree in afieldsuch asbusiness.
certifications: Insurance sales agents must have a license in the states where they work. Separate licenses are required for agents to sell life and health insurance and property and casualty insurance. In most states, licenses are issued only to applicants who complete specified courses and who pass state exams covering insurance fundamentals and state insurance laws. Most state licensing authorities also require agents to take continuing education courses focusing on topics such as insurance laws, consumer protection, ethics, and the technical details of various insurance policies. Some insurance sales agents also sell securities and other financial products. To do so, they must become licensed by theFinancial Industry Regulatory Authority(FINRA). FINRA's Series 6 exam is for agents who want to sell financial products, such as municipal fund securities, mutual funds, and variable annuities. Its Series 7 exam is the main FINRA series license, which qualifies agents as general securities sales representatives. A number of organizations offer certifications that show an agent’s expertise in insurance specialties. These certifications are not required for employment, but they may give job candidates an advantage over other applicants.

industries.name: Direct insurance (except life, health, and medical) carriers
Direct health and medical insurance carriers

title: Insurance underwriters
exposure_rationale: Insurance underwriting is almost entirely knowledge work done at a computer — analyzing data, evaluating risk factors, and making coverage decisions — all of which are core AI strengths. The BLS itself notes that automated underwriting software is already reducing the need for human underwriters, with employment projected to decline, and AI/ML models are increasingly capable of handling even complex risk assessments that previously required human judgment. The only partial barriers are edge cases requiring nuanced human judgment, regulatory accountability, and occasional physical property inspections, but these represent a small fraction of the work.

ai_paths.reason: Evaluates risk and property value using similar analytical frameworks.
Transfers risk assessment skills to project oversight and budgeting.
Leverages deep product knowledge to advise clients on coverage.
Uses objective evaluation and conflict resolution to settle disputes.
Analyzes financial data to determine risk and provide guidance.
Pivots risk mitigation expertise to public safety and planning.
Identifies workplace hazards to minimize liability and insurance risk.

education_detail: Employers usually prefer to hire candidates who have a bachelor’s degree. A commonfield of degreeisbusiness. Coursework in finance, economics, and mathematics is helpful. Some colleges and universities partner with local businesses to offer internships. These opportunities allow students to gain knowledge or practical experience through assisting in a variety of tasks, such as underwriting.
certifications: Employers may expect underwriters to become certified through coursework. These courses are important for keeping current with new insurance policies and changes in state and federal regulations. Many options are available for certification or insurance specialty designations. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Requirements for certification or designation vary and often include coursework or exams or both. Some credentials are available to new underwriters, but others require candidates to have a specified number of years of experience.

industries.name: Direct insurance (except life, health, and medical) carriers
Direct health and medical insurance carriers

title: Interior designers
exposure_rationale: Interior design involves substantial digital work — CAD/BIM software, space planning, material specification, cost estimation, and client presentations — all areas where AI tools are rapidly advancing and already useful. However, the job also requires significant physical presence (site visits, client relationship management, overseeing installations) and creative judgment that is deeply contextual and interpersonal. AI can accelerate visualization, mood boarding, and specification tasks considerably, but the blend of physical site work, client collaboration, and regulatory navigation keeps this from being a purely digital role.

ai_paths.reason: Overseeing spatial projects and coordinating with various building trades.
Applying knowledge of building codes and structural design standards.
Utilizing aesthetic principles, color theory, and spatial arrangement.
Instructing students in specialized design and drafting skills.
Designing activities and environments to improve client well-being.

education_detail: Interior designers entering the occupation typically need a bachelor’sdegreein any field, withfine artssubjects being most common. Studies should include courses in interior design, drawing, and computer-aided design (CAD). Programs in interior design are available at the associate’s, bachelor’s, and master’s degree levels. Applicants to these programs may need to submit sketches and other examples of their artistic ability.
certifications: Licensure requirements vary by state. In some states, only licensed designers may do interior design work. In other states, both licensed and unlicensed designers may do such work; however, only licensed designers may use the title “interior designer.” In still other states, both licensed and unlicensed designers may call themselves interior designers and do interior design work. In states with laws restricting the use of the title ”interior designer,” only candidates who pass their state-approved exam may call themselves registered interior designers. Candidate eligibility for taking the exam includes having at least a bachelor’s degree in interior design and 2 years of full-time work experience. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Voluntary certification in an interior design specialty, such as environmental design, allows designers to demonstrate expertise in a particular area of the occupation. Interior designers often specialize to distinguish the type of design work they do and to promote their expertise. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Interpreters and translators
exposure_rationale: Translation is fundamentally a digital, text-based task where AI (especially large language models) has already achieved near-human quality for many language pairs, and machine translation post-editing is already standard practice in the industry. Written translation faces the highest displacement risk, as AI tools can handle large volumes rapidly with human review. Interpreting has more resilience due to real-time human presence requirements, cultural nuance, and specialized domains (legal, medical, sign language), but even here remote AI-assisted interpreting is advancing quickly, explaining the already-slow projected job growth.
education_detail: Interpreters and translators typically need a bachelor’s degree; commonfields of degreeincludeforeign language,business, andcommunications. Students who study technical subjects, such as engineering or medicine, may be able to provide a higher level of interpreting and translation. Interpreters and translators also need to be proficient in at least two languages, one of which is usually English, and in the translation or interpretation skill they plan to provide. High school students interested in becoming an interpreter or translator should take a broad range of classes, including in foreign languages and English. Through community organizations, students interested in sign language interpreting may take introductory classes in American Sign Language (ASL) and seek out volunteer opportunities to work with people who are deaf or hard of hearing. Internships offer prospective interpreters and translators an opportunity to learn about the work. For example, interns may shadow an experienced interpreter or begin working in industries with particularly high demand for language services, such as court or medical interpreting.
certifications: General certification typically is not required for interpreters and translators. However, workers may show proficiency by passing a variety of optional certification tests. Employers may require or prefer certification for some types of interpreters and translators. For example, most states require certification for court interpreters. Federal courts offer court interpreter certification in several languages, including Spanish, Navajo, and Haitian Creole. At the state level, courts offer certification in multiple languages. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. TheU.S. Department of Stateoffers aptitude tests for interpreters and translators at various levels, from basic to advanced. Although these tests are not considered a credential, they are a required step for candidates to be added to a roster for freelance assignments. Other federal agencies may offer similar proficiency tests.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private
Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Ironworkers
exposure_rationale: Ironworking is fundamentally a physical, hands-on trade requiring workers to lift, cut, bend, weld, and connect heavy steel and iron components at construction sites, often at great heights and in variable conditions. AI has essentially no ability to perform these core tasks, which demand physical strength, balance, depth perception, and real-time adaptation to unpredictable environments. Minor peripheral tasks like blueprint reading or scheduling could see some AI assistance, but the vast majority of the job remains firmly in the physical domain.
education_detail: A high school diploma or equivalent is generally required to enter an apprenticeship. Workers learning through on-the-job training may not need a high school diploma or equivalent. Courses in math, as well as training in vocational subjects such as blueprint reading and welding, are useful.
certifications: Certifications in welding, rigging, and crane signaling may make ironworkers more attractive to prospective employers. Several organizations provide certifications for different aspects of the work. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Foundation, structure, and building exterior contractors

title: Janitors and building cleaners
exposure_rationale: Janitorial work is almost entirely physical and hands-on — sweeping, mopping, cleaning restrooms, lifting supplies, making minor repairs — tasks that require physical presence and cannot be done remotely or digitally. While robotic cleaning equipment (autonomous floor scrubbers, etc.) exists and may gradually displace some workers, this is more automation than AI per se, and the unpredictable, varied physical environments still require human adaptability. AI has essentially no impact on the core daily tasks of this occupation.
education_detail: Janitors and building cleaners typically do not need a formal educational credential to qualify for entry-level jobs. But for some positions, they may need to have a high school diploma or equivalent. Elective high school courses, such as in industrial arts, may be helpful for occupations involving repair.
certifications: Although not required, certification demonstrates competence and may make applicants more appealing to employers. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private
Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Jewelers and precious stone and metal workers
exposure_rationale: Jewelry work is fundamentally a hands-on, manual craft requiring fine motor dexterity, physical tools, and tactile skill to shape metals, set stones, and repair pieces — tasks AI cannot directly perform. While AI and CAD/CAM tools can assist with design and 3D printing is already automating some production steps (contributing to the projected employment decline), the core bench work remains physical and artisanal. The gemology and appraisal aspects have some knowledge-work component that AI could assist with, but the dominant share of the occupation is physical craftsmanship.
education_detail: Jewelers and precious stone and metal workers typically need a high school diploma, although some employers hire candidates who do not have formal education and others may require or prefer education beyond high school. Trade schools offer postsecondary education for prospective jewelers and precious stone and metal workers. Programs may include courses such as introduction to gems and metals, resizing, repair, and computer-aided design (CAD). Students may learn how to design, cast, set, and polish jewelry and gems, as well as how to use and care for a jeweler’s tools and equipment.
certifications: Jewelers and precious stone and metal workers may choose to earn professional certification. Although typically optional, certification demonstrates expertise in a particular area of work, such as gemology.

industries.name: Jewelry, luggage, and leather goods retailers
Personal and household goods repair and maintenance

title: Judges and hearing officers
exposure_rationale: Judges and hearing officers perform substantial knowledge work — researching legal issues, reading briefs, writing opinions, and applying precedent — that AI can meaningfully assist with, pushing exposure upward. However, the role is fundamentally anchored in physical courtroom presence, human judgment on credibility and fairness, constitutional authority, and public trust that society is unlikely to delegate to AI systems. The institutional, political, and ethical barriers to AI replacing judicial decision-making are exceptionally high, even as AI tools could significantly augment research and drafting tasks.

ai_paths.reason: Expertise in criminal law and evaluating evidence for justice.
Applying regulatory compliance and high-level decision-making to projects.
Analyzing facts and applying legal codes to determine liability.
Interpreting complex regulations to ensure strict legal compliance.
Impartial rule enforcement and making definitive split-second judgments.

education_detail: A Juris Doctor (J.D.) degree is typically required for jobs as a local, state, or federal judge or hearing officer. Earning a law degree usually takes 7 years of full-time study after high school: 4 years of undergraduate study in anyfield, followed by 3 years of law school. Law degree programs include courses such as constitutional law, contracts, property law, civil procedure, and legal writing. Although a J.D. is typical, requirements for these positions may vary. Hearing officers, magistrates, and even judges in some jurisdictions are not required to have a law degree.
certifications: Judges typically must maintain a law license and good standing with their state bar association.

industries.name: State government, excluding education and hospitals
Local government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Kindergarten and elementary school teachers
exposure_rationale: Elementary teaching involves significant physical presence, real-time human interaction, and relationship-building with young children that AI cannot replicate — supervising recess, managing classroom behavior, and nurturing social-emotional development are inherently human tasks. However, AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, grading, generating instructional materials, and personalizing learning content, making teachers more productive in their knowledge-work tasks. The core of the job remains anchored in physical presence and interpersonal care for children, limiting AI's ability to fully restructure the occupation even as it becomes a useful tool.
education_detail: Public kindergarten and elementary school teachers typically need a bachelor'sdegreein elementaryeducation. Private schools typically have the same requirement. In some states, public schools also require these teachers to major in a content area, such asmathematics. Those with a bachelor’s degree in another subject can still become elementary education teachers. They must complete a teacher education program to obtain certification to teach. Requirements vary by state. In teacher education programs, future teachers learn how to present information to young students and how to work with young students of varying abilities and backgrounds. Programs typically include a student-teaching program, in which they work with a mentor teacher and get experience teaching students in a classroom setting. Some states require teachers to earn a master’s degree after receiving their teaching certification and obtaining a job.
certifications: All states require teachers in public schools to be licensed or certified in the specific grade level that they will teach. Those who teach in private schools typically do not need a license. Requirements for certification or licensure vary by state but generally involve the following: A bachelor’s degree with a minimum grade point averageCompletion of a student teaching programPassing a background checkPassing a general teaching certification test, as well as a test that demonstrates their knowledge of the subject they will teach. Teachers are frequently required to complete professional development classes to keep their license or certification. Some states require teachers to complete a master’s degree after receiving their certification and obtaining a job. All states offer an alternative route to certification or licensure for people who already have a bachelor’s degree but lack the education courses required for certification. Some alternative certification programs allow candidates to begin teaching immediately after graduation, under the supervision of an experienced teacher. These programs cover teaching methods and child development. After they complete the program, candidates are awarded full certification. Other programs require students to take classes in education before they can teach.

title: Labor relations specialists
exposure_rationale: Labor relations specialists do substantial knowledge work — drafting contracts, analyzing labor law, writing policies, researching grievances — that AI can meaningfully assist with or partially automate. However, the core of the job involves high-stakes interpersonal negotiation, dispute resolution, and building trust between adversarial parties, which requires human judgment and relationship skills that AI cannot replicate. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level where AI tools will boost individual productivity (e.g., contract drafting, compliance research) but the irreducibly human elements of mediation and negotiation provide a meaningful buffer.

ai_paths.reason: Expertise in managing contracts and resolving site-level disputes.
Strong investigative skills and experience with legal compliance.
Applying organizational leadership and staff conflict resolution skills.
Leveraging advocacy experience to assist vulnerable populations navigate systems.
Teaching specialized workplace skills and professional standards to students.
Ensuring workplace regulatory compliance and investigating safety grievances.

education_detail: Labor relations specialists typically need a bachelor’s degree. Some schools offer a bachelor’s degree in labor and industrial relations. Candidates also may qualify for labor relations specialist positions with a bachelor’sdegreein a field such as human resources orbusiness. Coursework for these majors typically includes business, human resource management, and accounting. Some colleges and universities offer labor relations certificates in specialized topics, such as mediation. Earning these certificates gives participants a better understanding of labor law, the collective bargaining process, and worker grievance procedures. College students who participate in internships or job shadowing, such as with an organization’s human resources department, may have an opportunity to gain insight into labor relations while still in school.
certifications: Although not required, employers may prefer to hire candidates who have professional certification. Certification demonstrates a level of proficiency in the skills required for the occupation. For labor relations specialists, human resources credentials may either be general or cover specific workforce topics.

industries.name: Labor unions and similar labor organizations
Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Landscape architects
exposure_rationale: Landscape architecture is a mix of digital knowledge work (CADD design, GIS analysis, cost estimation, client presentations, environmental analysis) and physical site work (inspections, material selection, on-site coordination). AI tools can significantly assist with the design, visualization, and analytical components — generative design, automated drafting, environmental modeling — potentially making individual architects much more productive. However, the job retains meaningful physical presence requirements, creative judgment about natural environments, and client relationship management that limit full automation.
education_detail: Landscape architects typically need a bachelor's or master'sdegreein landscape architecture or a related field, such asarchitecture. There are two undergraduate landscape architect degrees: a Bachelor of Landscape Architecture (BLA) and a Bachelor of Science in Landscape Architecture (BSLA). These programs usually require 4 to 5 years of study. Prospective landscape architects whose undergraduate degree is in another field may enroll in a Master of Landscape Architecture (MLA) graduate degree program, which typically takes 3 years of full-time study. Courses typically include landscape design and construction, landscape ecology, and site design. Other relevant coursework may include history of landscape architecture, plant and soil science, and professional practice. The design studio is a key component of any curriculum. When possible, students are assigned projects that offer hands-on experience. These projects allow students to work with computer-aided design and drafting (CADD), model building, and other design software.
certifications: All states require landscape architects to be licensed. Candidates for licensure must pass a licensing exam. Candidates who are interested in taking the exam usually need a degree from an accredited school and experience working under the supervision of a licensed landscape architect, although standards vary by state. For candidates without a degree in landscape architecture, many states offer alternative paths—which usually require more work experience—to qualify to take the licensing exam. In addition to the licensing exam, some states have their own registration exam to test for competency on state-specific issues, such as earthquakes in California or hurricanes in Florida. State-specific exams may focus on laws, environmental regulations, plants, soils, climate, and other characteristics unique to the state. Licensed landscape architects also may obtain voluntary certification, which might make it easier to get licensed in another state. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Lawyers
exposure_rationale: Legal work is predominantly knowledge-based — research, document drafting, contract review, and legal analysis — all of which are domains where AI is rapidly advancing and already demonstrating strong capability. However, core lawyering involves significant human elements: courtroom advocacy, client relationships, negotiation, and the exercise of judgment in high-stakes, nuanced situations that still require human presence and accountability. The BLS itself notes that 'some routine legal work may be automated,' and AI tools like contract review, legal research, and document generation are already reshaping the profession, but the irreducibly interpersonal and adversarial nature of legal practice provides a meaningful buffer against full automation.
education_detail: Becoming a lawyer usually takes 7 years of full-time study after high school: 4 years of undergraduate study followed by 3 years of law school. Although most law schools do not require a specific bachelor'sdegreefor entry, common undergraduate fields of study includelaw and legal studies,history, andsocial science. Most states and jurisdictions require lawyers to earn a Juris Doctor (J.D.) degree from an accredited law school. Accredited programs include courses such as constitutional law, contracts, property law, civil procedure, and legal writing. As part of their admissions process, law schools may consider an applicant’s score on the national exam. Questions on this exam cover reasoning, writing, and other aptitudes needed for the study of law. Those interested in pursuing a career in some legal fields may need to meet additional requirements. For example, patent lawyers typically need a degree, specific credits, or a background in science or engineering and must pass an exam administered by the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (commonly known as the patent bar exam). Tax lawyers may choose to earn a Master of Laws (LL.M) degree in tax after completing a J.D. program.
certifications: Prospective lawyers take a licensing exam, called the “bar exam.” A score from the exam is transferable across jurisdictions that accept it. Lawyers who receive a license to practice law are “admitted to the bar.” Each state’s highest court establishes its rules for bar admission. Rules for federal courts differ, and requirements vary by state and jurisdiction. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Most states require that applicants graduate from an accredited law school, pass the written bar exam, and be found by an admitting board to have the character to represent and advise others. Prior felony convictions, academic misconduct, and a history of substance abuse are examples of factors that may disqualify an applicant from being admitted to the bar. Lawyers who want to practice in more than one state usually must meet licensing requirements for each state in which they wish to work. Most states have reciprocity agreements that streamline the process for lawyers licensed in one state to get licensed in another state. After bar admission, lawyers must keep informed about legal developments that affect their practice. States may require lawyers to participate in continuing legal education to maintain licensure.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals
State government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Librarians and library media specialists
exposure_rationale: Librarians do substantial knowledge work — research assistance, database management, collection curation, and information organization — that AI can increasingly handle, and AI-powered search tools directly threaten the core reference and research-assistance functions. However, significant portions of the role involve physical presence (managing physical collections, running community programs, working with patrons face-to-face, supervising staff), and the job has strong institutional and community-service dimensions that resist full automation. The slow projected job growth suggests AI and digital resources are already suppressing demand, but the interpersonal, educational, and community-programming aspects provide meaningful insulation.

ai_paths.reason: Strong educational leadership and program administration skills.
Experience planning community programs and engaging diverse populations.
Adapting information and instructional materials for diverse learners.
Connecting community members with vital information and services.
Teaching information literacy and specialized research skills.
Public outreach and navigating complex information systems.

education_detail: Librarians typically need a master'sdegreeinlibrary science. Some colleges and universities have other names for their library science programs, such as Master of Information Studies or Master of Library and Information Studies. Students need a bachelor’s degree in any major to enter MLS or similar programs. MLS programs usually take 1 to 2 years to complete. Coursework typically covers information such as learning different research methods and strategies, online reference systems, and Internet search techniques. Requirements for public school librarians and library media specialists vary by state. Most states require an MLS or a bachelor’s or master’s degree in education, often with a specialization related to library media. Special librarians, such as those in a corporate, law, or medical library, usually supplement a master’s degree in library science with knowledge of their specialized field. Some employers require special librarians to have a master’s degree, a professional degree, or a Ph.D. in that subject. For example, a law librarian may be required to have a law degree.
certifications: Public school librarians and library media specialists typically need a teacher’s certification. Some states require school librarians to pass a standardized test, such as the PRAXIS II Library Media Specialist test. Contact your state department of education for details about requirements in your state. Some states also require certification for librarians in public libraries. Contact your state’s licensing board for specific requirements.
