title: Surveying and mapping technicians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork (visiting sites, operating instruments, navigating terrain) and digital/knowledge work (GIS data processing, CAD software, map creation). AI and automation (drones, improved sensors) are already streamlining data collection, and AI tools can increasingly assist with image processing, map updating, and data integration. However, the significant physical presence required for fieldwork, equipment operation, and site verification creates a natural barrier to full AI displacement, keeping exposure moderate rather than high.

ai_paths.reason: Leverages spatial awareness and familiarity with construction sites.
Utilizes technical troubleshooting and precision measurement tools.
Applies land navigation and outdoor data gathering skills.
Uses precision alignment and technical equipment maintenance skills.

education_detail: Surveying and mapping technicians typically need a high school diploma, but some employers prefer to hire candidates who have some additional education. Postsecondary training or experience in GIS technology also may be helpful. High school students interested in working as a surveying or mapping technician should take classes in mathematics, such as algebra, geometry, and trigonometry. High school or postsecondary coursework in drafting, mechanical drawing, and computer science also may be useful.
certifications: Optional certification may help surveying and mapping technicians demonstrate competency in a relevant specialty. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Employers may prefer to hire candidates who have a valid driver’s license.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Surveyors
exposure_rationale: Surveying is a hybrid occupation with significant fieldwork requiring physical presence, travel to remote locations, and hands-on measurement in unpredictable terrain, which limits AI's direct substitution. However, the office-based components — processing GPS/GIS data, preparing maps and legal documents, analyzing records, and using CAD software — are increasingly automatable, and drone/remote sensing technologies are already noted as limiting employment growth. AI can substantially augment productivity in data interpretation and mapping tasks, but the licensed, legally accountable, and physically present nature of boundary certification creates a meaningful barrier to full displacement.

ai_paths.reason: Mechanical aptitude from maintaining complex electronic surveying instruments.
Strong background in reading technical drawings and spatial alignment.
Precision measurement skills and ability to interpret complex schematics.

education_detail: Surveyors typically need a bachelor’sdegreein land surveying or a related field, such asengineeringornatural resources. Some colleges and universities offer programs that prepare students to become licensed surveyors. In some cases, employers may hire candidates who have an associate’s degree and additional training.
certifications: All 50 states and the District of Columbia require surveyors to be licensed before they can provide their services to the public. These services include certifying legal documents that show property lines or determine proper markings, such as for construction projects. Prospective licensed surveyors may need a bachelor’s degree from an accredited program in order to sit for the licensing exam. Candidates typically work for several years under the direction of a licensed surveyor to qualify for licensure. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Tax examiners and collectors, and revenue agents
exposure_rationale: Tax examination and revenue work is predominantly knowledge-based — reviewing financial documents, analyzing tax returns, interpreting regulations, and making determinations — all of which are tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. AI can already automate much of the routine return review, anomaly detection, and compliance checking that forms the core of this work. However, the job retains meaningful human elements such as negotiating with taxpayers, conducting field audits, exercising judgment in complex cases, and navigating sensitive interpersonal situations with delinquent taxpayers, which moderates the score somewhat.

ai_paths.reason: Applying valuation principles and tax law to property assessments.
Resolving disputes using legal knowledge and negotiation skills.
Enforcing compliance through case management and detailed documentation.
Interpreting complex contracts and ensuring regulatory compliance.
Conducting financial background checks and verifying factual information.
Analyzing financial data to assist with debt management.
Evaluating adherence to codes and maintaining detailed records.

education_detail: Tax examiners and collectors, and revenue agents typically need a bachelor’sdegreein accounting or a related field, such asbusiness. For some jobs, work experience may substitute for a degree. Candidates for tax examiner and collector positions at theInternal Revenue Service(IRS) may qualify with a bachelor’s degree in any field of study or with specialized experience, or with a combination of education and experience. Internal revenue agents at the IRS generally need a bachelor’s degree in accounting; a combination of education and experience equivalent to a major in accounting; or a Certified Public Accountant (CPA) certificate.

title: Taxi drivers, shuttle drivers, and chauffeurs
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical — driving a vehicle through real-world environments — which creates a natural barrier to AI automation in the near term. However, autonomous vehicle technology (Waymo, Tesla, etc.) represents a long-term existential threat that could eventually displace most of these workers, making the indirect AI exposure significant. The BLS still projects strong growth through 2034, suggesting the transition is not imminent, but the trajectory of self-driving technology means this occupation faces more AI-driven disruption than most physical jobs.
education_detail: Taxi drivers, shuttle drivers, and chauffeurs typically do not need formal educational credentials to enter the occupation. For drivers who are not self-employed, however, companies may prefer to hire drivers who have a high school diploma or postsecondary education.
certifications: All taxi drivers, shuttle drivers, and chauffeurs must have a driver’s license. States and local municipalities set other requirements; check with your state or local agency for more information. Taxi drivers and chauffeurs may need a taxi or limousine license. TheFederal Motor Carrier Safety Administrationrequires drivers who transport 16 or more passengers (including the driver) to hold a commercial driver’s license (CDL) with a passenger (P) endorsement. Licensure normally requires passing a background check, drug test, driving skills test, and written exam about regulations and local geography. Regulations for ride-hailing drivers vary by state and city.

title: Teacher assistants
exposure_rationale: Teacher assistants perform predominantly in-person, hands-on work including supervising students, providing physical assistance to special needs students, and building interpersonal relationships — all of which require physical presence and human connection. While AI could assist with some peripheral tasks like recordkeeping or grading, the core duties of one-on-one student support, behavioral guidance, and physical care are highly resistant to automation. The occupation's projected slight decline likely reflects budget pressures more than AI displacement, and the irreplaceable human element in working with children and students with disabilities keeps AI exposure low.
education_detail: Teacher assistants in public schools need at least 2 years of college coursework or an associate’s degree. Those who work in schools with a Title 1 program (a federal program for schools that have a large proportion of students from low-income households) must have at least a 2-year degree, 2 years of college, or pass a state or local assessment. Associate’s degree programs for teacher assistants prepare participants to develop educational materials, observe students, and understand the role of teaching assistants in working with classroom teachers. Some teacher assistants have a bachelor's degree infieldssuch aseducationandpsychology. Most states require teacher assistants who work with special-needs students to pass a skills test.
certifications: Some jobs may require staff to have certifications in cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and first aid.

title: Technical writers
exposure_rationale: Technical writing is almost entirely digital knowledge work — researching, drafting, editing, and structuring text-based documentation — which sits squarely in AI's strongest domain. LLMs are already capable of generating first drafts of manuals, how-to guides, and FAQs, and can rapidly synthesize technical specifications into plain language. The BLS itself notes that AI tools are slowing employment growth by boosting individual productivity, and the projected 1% growth over a decade reflects this displacement pressure, with the occupation's small size making even modest AI-driven efficiency gains highly impactful.

ai_paths.reason: Translating complex information into structured educational curricula and materials.
Creating manuals and instructional content to improve employee skills.
Explaining technical concepts and specialized knowledge to diverse audiences.
Organizing complex documentation and ensuring regulatory compliance in healthcare.
Communicating specialized organizational information to the public effectively.
Applying technical aptitude to operate and document media equipment.
Developing safety procedures and documenting technical regulatory standards.
Breaking down complex subject matter for student comprehension.

education_detail: Employers generally prefer candidates who have a bachelor’sdegreeinEnglish,communications, journalism, or a related field. Technical writing jobs may require candidates to have knowledge of a technical field, such asengineeringorcomputer and information technology.
certifications: Some associations offer certification for technical writers. Although not mandatory, these credentials demonstrate competence and professionalism, making candidates more attractive to employers. A professional credential also may increase a technical writer’s opportunities for advancement. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Telecommunications technicians
exposure_rationale: Telecommunications technicians perform predominantly physical, hands-on work — climbing towers, splicing fiber optic cables, installing equipment in homes and central offices — which creates a strong natural barrier to AI automation. AI can assist with remote diagnostics, network monitoring, and troubleshooting guidance, but the core job requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and on-site problem-solving. The occupation is already projected to decline slightly due to technological improvements in infrastructure durability rather than AI displacement specifically.
education_detail: Telecommunications technicians typically need at least a high school diploma or equivalent. Employers of telecommunications equipment installers and repairers and radio, cellular, and tower equipment installers and repairers may prefer to hire candidates who have some postsecondary education in electronics, telecommunications, or computer networking. Some employers prefer to hire candidates who have an associate’s degree. Community colleges and technical schools offer courses in subjects such as data transmission systems, data communication, AC/DC electrical circuits, and computer programming. These courses typically are included in programs that lead to a certificate or an associate’s degree in telecommunications or related subjects.
certifications: Manufacturer or employer-provided certification may be helpful for some telecom technicians. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Telecom technicians may need a driver’s license to travel to jobsites, and workers who drive heavy vehicles usually need a commercial driver’s license (CDL). Some employers prefer or require that candidates have certification in first aid or in cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR).

title: Tellers
exposure_rationale: Bank teller work is already being heavily disrupted by digital banking, ATMs, and automation — the BLS projects a 13% employment decline over the next decade. The core tasks (processing transactions, verifying accounts, handling cash) are increasingly handled by ATMs, mobile banking apps, and AI-driven systems, while the remaining human role shifts toward complex customer service interactions. The job has a physical/interpersonal component that provides some barrier, but the transaction-processing and information-handling aspects are highly automatable, and AI-enhanced kiosks and virtual tellers are already reducing headcount per branch.

ai_paths.reason: Precision in handling regulated items and customer transactions.
Expertise in cash handling and high-stakes customer interaction.
Experience with secure processing and public-facing service duties.
Managing financial records and maintaining professional client relationships.
Providing personalized service and managing detailed client requests.
Serving as a trusted point of contact for community members.

education_detail: Tellers usually need a high school diploma or the equivalent. Some tellers take college courses or have a college degree, but this is not typically required for a job applicant to be hired.

title: Top executives
exposure_rationale: Top executives perform substantial knowledge work — analyzing reports, formulating strategy, and communicating decisions — that AI can increasingly assist with, pushing exposure upward. However, the core value of executive roles lies in human judgment, relationship-building, accountability, negotiation, and leadership in unpredictable organizational contexts, which AI cannot replicate. AI will likely make executives more productive by automating analysis and drafting tasks, but the interpersonal, political, and trust-based dimensions of leadership create a meaningful ceiling on displacement.

ai_paths.reason: Strategic oversight, budgeting, and high-level project leadership skills transfer.
Operational leadership, P&L management, and staff coordination experience.

education_detail: Top executives typically need a bachelor's or master'sdegreein an area related to their field of work, such asbusinessorengineering. Top executives in the public sector may have a degree in business administration, public administration, law, or the liberal arts. Top executives of large corporations may have a master’s degree in business administration (MBA). College presidents and school superintendents are typically required to have a master’s degree, although a doctorate is often preferred. Although many mayors, governors, and other public sector executives have at least a bachelor’s degree, these positions typically do not have any specific education requirements.
certifications: Some top executive positions may require the applicant to have a license or certification relevant to their area of management. For example, some employers may require their chief executive officer to be a certified public accountant (CPA).

title: Tour and travel guides
exposure_rationale: Tour and travel guides work is fundamentally physical and interpersonal — leading groups through locations, adapting to real-time situations, and building rapport with clients in person. While AI could assist with research, itinerary planning, and logistics, the core value of the job lies in live human presence, storytelling, and dynamic interaction that AI cannot replicate in the field. Some peripheral tasks like reservation management or content research may be streamlined by AI, but the occupation's growth trajectory and inherently embodied nature limit overall exposure.
education_detail: Tour and travel guides usually need a high school diploma or the equivalent, although entry requirements may vary. High school students interested in becoming a tour or travel guide may benefit from taking classes such as history, art, or foreign languages. Some tour and travel guides choose to pursue a bachelor’sdegree. Although there is no standard degree program , common fields of degree includebusiness,education, andfine and performing arts. Fluency in more than one language may be useful for tour and travel guides.
certifications: Tour and travel guides in some locations, such as New York City and Washington, D.C., may need a locally issued license. Cruise directors must have aTransportation Worker Identification Credential(TWIC) from theTransportation Security Administration(TSA) to work onboard the ship. Some tour and travel guides need to have a valid driver’s license or a commercial driver’s license (CDL) if they drive for bus tours. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

AI can accelerate content creation, needs analysis, and program evaluation, but the interpersonal leadership and organizational navigation components provide a meaningful buffer against full automation.

exposure_rationale: Training and development specialists do substantial knowledge work — designing curricula, creating learning materials, assessing needs, and evaluating programs — all areas where AI can meaningfully assist or partially automate. However, a significant portion of the role involves live facilitation, interpersonal coaching, and collaborative delivery that requires human presence and relationship-building. Utilizes experience in conducting safety training and compliance workshops.
Transfers skills in designing goal-oriented activities and assessing progress.
Applies interpersonal communication and group facilitation for community support.

education_detail: Training and development specialists typically need a bachelor’s degree, often in abusinessfield such as organizational development or human resources. Otherfields of degreeincludeeducation,social science,psychology, andcommunications. Candidates who do not have a bachelor’s degree sometimes qualify for jobs if they have experience developing and delivering training. The experience may need to be extensive and specific to the employer’s industry.
certifications: Although not usually required, certification shows professional expertise and credibility. However, some employers prefer to hire certified candidates, and some positions require certification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private

title: Transportation, storage, and distribution managers
exposure_rationale: This role combines significant knowledge work (budgeting, planning, compliance monitoring, supply chain optimization) with substantial physical presence requirements (warehouse oversight, staff management, facility inspections) and real-time operational decision-making. AI can meaningfully assist with route optimization, inventory forecasting, compliance tracking, and logistics analytics, but the core managerial functions—supervising workers, resolving on-the-ground disruptions, negotiating with suppliers, and leading teams—still require human judgment and physical presence. The growing complexity of e-commerce supply chains may actually increase demand for these managers even as AI tools enhance their productivity.

ai_paths.reason: Transfers expertise in logistics, scheduling, and large-scale resource coordination.
Applies inventory control, supply chain oversight, and staff management.
Leverages deep knowledge of transport regulations and route optimization.
Uses technical understanding of warehouse equipment and mechanical systems.
Applies distribution planning and large-scale storage management expertise.
Utilizes experience monitoring shipping standards and inventory accuracy protocols.

education_detail: Transportation, storage, and distribution managers typically need at least a high school diploma, but education requirements for individual positions may vary. High school classes in subjects such as English, mathematics, and economics may be helpful for prospective transportation, storage, and distribution managers. Employers may prefer to hire candidates who have a bachelor’sdegreein a field such asbusiness,transportation, orengineering. Some colleges and universities offer bachelor's degree programs in supply chain management and logistics.
certifications: Some employers require or prefer that transportation, storage, and distribution managers have specific types of certifications, licenses, or both. Whether optional or required, certifications demonstrate competency in a particular area of transportation, storage, or distribution management. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Some transportation, storage, and distribution managers may need a driver's license or a commercial driver’s license (CDL) to travel to related warehouses and storage facilities or to assist with transportation operations. Depending on the type of good or facility being managed, some states have additional licensing requirements. Occupation-specific licenses, such as for engineers and marine captains, may be required of managers who oversee some types of workers.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals

title: Travel agents
exposure_rationale: Travel agents work primarily on computers and phones, researching destinations, booking reservations, and communicating with clients — all tasks where AI is already highly capable. AI-powered travel planning tools, chatbots, and booking platforms can automate much of the information-gathering, itinerary-building, and reservation-making that defines this role. However, the occupation retains some resilience through personalized client relationships, handling complex or emergency situations, and the value of firsthand destination experience that clients specifically seek out from human agents.

ai_paths.reason: Deep destination knowledge and itinerary planning skills transfer directly.
Expertise in customer service and managing complex travel logistics.
Understanding of hospitality industry operations and guest satisfaction.
Extensive travel industry experience and high-level customer service.
Strong sales background and experience managing high-value transactions.
Ability to coordinate complex logistics under time pressure.
Proven sales ability and experience explaining complex policy details.

education_detail: Travel agents typically need at least a high school diploma to enter the occupation, but employers may prefer to hire candidates who have a college degree or who have taken courses related to the travel industry. Community colleges, vocational schools, and industry associations may offer technical training, certificates, or continuing education in professional travel planning. In addition, some 4-year colleges offer degrees in travel and tourism. Courses usually focus on reservations systems, marketing, and regulations regarding international travel.
certifications: Some states require travel agents to have a business license, to register with the state, or to meet other conditions. Requirements vary by state. Contact individual state licensing agencies for more information. Although not required, some travel agents may choose to earn professional credentials. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Tutors
exposure_rationale: Tutoring involves significant human interaction, relationship-building, and adaptive instruction that AI cannot fully replicate, particularly for students with disabilities or those needing emotional support and motivation. However, the BLS itself notes that generative AI will reduce demand by automating many core tutoring tasks like explaining concepts, providing practice problems, and giving feedback — all fundamentally digital knowledge-transfer activities. The occupation sits at a moderate-to-high exposure level because while the interpersonal and adaptive elements provide some buffer, the core academic content delivery is highly susceptible to AI substitution.
education_detail: Tutors typically need to have some college education, specifically in subjects that they want to teach. Some tutors, such as those still in high school, may not have completed college courses but have a strong knowledge or background in a specific subject. Some employers require that tutors have a bachelor's degree in afieldsuch aseducation. Other employers may prefer to hire tutors whose degree relates closely to their tutoring subject, such asmathematicsor aforeign language. Depending on the position, tutors may receive training on the job.

industries.name: Colleges, universities, and professional schools; state

title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally physical and real-time, requiring officials to be present at sporting events, make split-second judgments based on direct observation, and manage interpersonal disputes with athletes and coaches. While AI and video replay technology can assist with some calls (e.g., hawk-eye systems, goal-line technology), the core job requires physical presence, situational awareness, and human authority that AI cannot replace. AI may incrementally assist with replay review but is unlikely to displace the need for on-field human officials in the foreseeable future.
education_detail: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials typically need a high school diploma, although requirements may vary. Each state and sport association has its own education requirements for umpires, referees, and other sports officials. Some do not require formal education, while others may require umpires, referees, and sports officials to have a high school diploma. Some sports, such as baseball, have their own professional training schools that prepare aspiring umpires and officials for a career at the minor and major league levels. For more information on educational requirements, refer to the specific state athletic or activity association.
certifications: Credentialing requirements vary by competition level. For example, to officiate high school athletic events, umpires, referees, and other officials must typically register with the state or local agency that oversees high school athletics. They also typically need to pass an exam on the rules of the particular sport. Some states and associations require applicants to attend umpiring or refereeing classes before taking the exam or joining the association. Other associations require officials to attend annual training workshops before renewing their officiating credential.

industries.name: Performing arts, spectator sports, and related industries
Educational services; state, local, and private

title: Urban and regional planners
exposure_rationale: Urban and regional planners do substantial knowledge work — data analysis, GIS mapping, report writing, feasibility assessments — that AI can increasingly assist with or automate, pushing exposure upward. However, a significant portion of the job involves stakeholder engagement, public meetings, political negotiation, and site visits that require human presence and interpersonal judgment. AI tools can meaningfully accelerate the analytical and documentation tasks, but the community-facing, regulatory, and relationship-management dimensions create a natural ceiling on full automation.

ai_paths.reason: Overseeing site development and coordinating complex infrastructure projects.
Applying regulatory knowledge to monitor and protect land use.
Mapping and measuring land for legal and planning purposes.
Engaging diverse populations to improve local living conditions.
Managing natural resources and evaluating land-use impacts.
Ensuring public environments meet strict regulatory and safety standards.

education_detail: Urban and regional planners typically need a master'sdegreein urban and regional planning or a related field. Admission to master's degree programs typically requires a bachelor’s degree. Programs usually do not specify a particular undergraduate degree for admission, but students may benefit from having a background inarchitecture,social science,business, or a related field. Accredited programs typically include coursework in subjects such as planning theory, land use law, and GIS for planning. Programs often have seminars and workshops, in which students learn to analyze and solve planning problems, and may offer concentrations in rural planning, urban revitalization, or other disciplines.
certifications: Most states do not require urban and regional planners to be licensed. New Jersey is an exception; information about its licensing requirements is available from theregulatory board of New Jersey. Employers may prefer or require urban and regional planners to be certified. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals
State government, excluding education and hospitals
Management, scientific, and technical consulting services

title: Veterinarians
exposure_rationale: Veterinarians perform substantial physical, hands-on work including surgery, wound treatment, physical examinations, and operating medical equipment that AI cannot replicate. However, AI can meaningfully assist with diagnostic support, medical record analysis, treatment planning, and interpreting imaging results, making the knowledge-work portions of the job more efficient. The core of the occupation remains grounded in physical presence, manual dexterity, and direct animal care, creating a natural ceiling on AI's ability to reshape the role fundamentally.
education_detail: Veterinarians must complete a Doctor of Veterinary Medicine (DVM or VMD) degree at an accredited college of veterinary medicine. A veterinary medicine program generally takes 4 years to complete and includes classroom, laboratory, and clinical components. Admission to veterinary programs is competitive. Applicants to veterinary school typically have a bachelor'sdegreein a field such asbiology. Veterinary medical colleges typically require applicants to have taken many science classes, including biology, chemistry, and animal science. Most programs also require math, humanities, and social science courses. Some veterinary medical colleges prefer candidates who have studiedagricultureor have experience working with animals on a farm, at a stable, or in an animal shelter. In veterinary medicine programs, students take courses on animal anatomy and physiology, as well as disease prevention, diagnosis, and treatment. Most programs include 3 years of classroom, laboratory, and clinical work. Students typically spend the final year of the 4-year program doing clinical rotations in a veterinary medical center or hospital.
certifications: Veterinarians must be licensed in order to practice in the United States. Licensing requirements vary by state, but prospective veterinarians in all states must complete an accredited veterinary program and pass a qualifying exam. In addition to passing the national exam, most states require that veterinarians pass a state licensing exam. However, veterinarians employed by state or federal government may not need a state license, because government agencies differ in what they require. Each state’s exam covers its laws and regulations. Few states accept licenses from other states, so veterinarians usually must take exams for the states in which they want to be licensed. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Although certification is not mandatory, it allows workers to demonstrate competency in animal husbandry, health and welfare, and facility administration. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Government, excluding state and local education and hospitals
Educational services; state, local, and private

title: Veterinary assistants and laboratory animal caretakers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is overwhelmingly physical and hands-on — feeding, bathing, restraining, and caring for animals in person — with essentially no core tasks that can be digitized or automated by AI. AI might marginally assist with scheduling, record-keeping, or monitoring systems, but these are peripheral to the job's primary duties. The high injury rate and physical demands underscore how deeply rooted this work is in real-world, unpredictable animal interactions.
education_detail: Most workers entering the occupation have a high school diploma or equivalent.
certifications: Although certification is not mandatory, it allows workers to demonstrate competency in animal husbandry, health and welfare, and facility administration. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private

title: Veterinary technologists and technicians
exposure_rationale: Veterinary technologists and technicians perform predominantly hands-on, physical work — restraining animals, administering anesthesia, collecting samples, providing nursing care — that requires physical presence and manual dexterity in unpredictable environments. AI may assist with diagnostic image analysis or record-keeping on the periphery, but the core job involves direct animal handling and real-time clinical judgment that cannot be automated. The occupation's high injury rate and physical demands underscore its fundamentally non-digital nature.
education_detail: Veterinary technologists usually have a 4-year bachelor’s degree in veterinary technology. Veterinary technicians usually have a 2-year associate’s degree in a veterinary technology program. Most of these programs offer a 2-year associate’s degree for veterinary technicians; others offer a 4-year bachelor’s degree for veterinary technologists. People interested in becoming a veterinary technologist or technician can prepare by taking biology and other science courses in high school.
certifications: Although each state regulates veterinary technologists and technicians differently, most states require technologists and technicians to pass an exam. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private

title: Waiters and waitresses
exposure_rationale: Waiting tables is fundamentally a physical, in-person job requiring carrying food, reading customer moods, and providing hospitality — tasks AI cannot directly perform. Some peripheral tasks like order-taking are being automated via kiosks and self-service technology, which explains the slight projected employment decline, but the core human interaction and physical service elements remain resistant to AI replacement. AI has minimal impact on the daily work of actually serving customers in a dining environment.
education_detail: Typically, no formal education is required to become a waiter or waitress. However, some employers require or prefer that workers have a high school diploma.

title: Water and wastewater treatment plant and system operators
exposure_rationale: This occupation involves significant physical presence, hands-on equipment operation, chemical handling, and on-site inspections that cannot be done remotely or digitally. However, AI and automation are already impacting the monitoring and control aspects — computerized systems increasingly manage treatment processes, contributing to the projected 7% employment decline. The core job still requires skilled workers physically present to respond to emergencies, maintain equipment, and operate complex systems, limiting AI exposure to moderate levels.
education_detail: Water and wastewater treatment plant and system operators typically need a high school diploma or equivalent to enter the occupation. High school students may benefit from taking classes in subjects such as biology, chemistry, and math. Employers may prefer applicants who have completed a certificate, an associate’s, or a bachelor’s degree program in a related field such as environmental science or wastewater treatment technology.
certifications: Water and wastewater treatment plant and system operators typically require a state license; however, entry-level workers often complete their licensing requirements during on-the-job training. Requirements and standards vary widely depending on the state. State licenses typically have multiple levels, which indicate the operator’s experience and training. Although some states will honor licenses from other states, operators who move from one state to another may need to take a new set of exams to become licensed in their new state. Information on state licensing board requirements is available fromCareerOneStop. Some workers choose to earn professional certification, which may help to demonstrate competency in the field.

industries.name: Local government, excluding education and hospitals

title: Water transportation workers
exposure_rationale: Water transportation work is fundamentally physical and place-bound, requiring workers to be aboard vessels operating in dynamic, unpredictable maritime environments. While AI could assist with navigation planning, route optimization, and some monitoring tasks, the core duties — operating vessels, maintaining machinery, managing cargo and passengers in real-time, and responding to emergencies at sea — require physical presence and hands-on skill that AI cannot replace. Autonomous shipping technology exists in early stages but faces enormous regulatory, safety, and technical barriers, keeping human crews essential for the foreseeable future.
education_detail: Sailors and marine oilers typically do not need a formal educational credential to enter the occupation. Other water transportation workers completeU.S. Coast Guardapproved training programs. Employers may prefer to hire candidates who have earned a bachelor’s degree from a maritime academy. For example, theU.S. Merchant Marine Academyoffers a bachelor’s degree program and a Merchant Mariner Credential (MMC) with an endorsement as a third mate or third assistant engineer. Academy graduates must serve several years as an officer in a reserve unit or on active duty in any branch of theArmed Forces.
certifications: The USCG and TSA have specific requirements for many water transportation workers. These requirements may include passing a security or drug screening, completing approved training, or passing written and physical examinations. Workers in some occupations, such as maritime pilots, also may need a license issued by their state. For example, mariners working on ships with U.S. flags usually need a TSATransportation Worker Identification Credential(TWIC). Mariners who work on ships traveling on the open ocean require USCG-verified training for the Standards of Training, Certification, and Watchkeeping (STCW) endorsement. Most mariners also must have a USCG-issued MMC. (More information on MMCs and related endorsements is available from theU.S. Coast Guard National Maritime Center.) In addition, water transportation workers may need a valid passport, other licenses or credentials, and first aid or other safety certification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Web developers and digital designers
exposure_rationale: Web development and digital design are almost entirely computer-based occupations where core tasks — writing code, designing interfaces, creating graphics, and testing applications — are precisely the domains where AI is advancing most rapidly. AI tools like GitHub Copilot, generative design platforms, and no-code/low-code builders are already substantially augmenting or replacing routine coding and design tasks, meaning fewer workers can produce the same output. While client communication, creative judgment, and complex problem-solving provide some buffer, the fundamentally digital nature of the work and the steep AI capability trajectory in coding and design place this occupation firmly in the very high exposure range.

ai_paths.reason: Technical proficiency with digital media and hardware interfaces.
Applying spatial aesthetics and user-centric design principles.
Designing structured digital learning experiences and curricula.
Translating 2D digital concepts into 3D environments.
Teaching technical skills and design theory to students.
Managing complex workflows and technical project lifecycles.
Troubleshooting complex systems using technical and analytical skills.
Utilizing creative software and understanding visual trends.

education_detail: Educational requirements for web developers and digital designers range from a high school diploma to a bachelor’s degree. Some employers prefer to hire web developer candidates who have a bachelor’s degree in a specific field, such as computer science or programming. Web developers need to have a thorough understanding of HTML programming. Many employers also want developers to understand other programming languages, such as JavaScript or SQL, and have knowledge of multimedia publishing tools, such as Flash. Throughout their career, web developers must keep up to date on new tools and computer languages. Employers of digital designers may prefer to hire candidates who have a bachelor’s degree in a field such as web design, digital design, or graphic arts. Web developers and digital designers may not need specific education credentials if they can demonstrate their abilities through prior work experience or projects.

industries.name: Computer systems design and related services
Advertising, public relations, and related services

title: Welders, cutters, solderers, and brazers
exposure_rationale: Welding is fundamentally a hands-on, physical trade requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and real-time judgment in variable environments — qualities that AI cannot replicate directly. While robotic welding automation exists in manufacturing, this affects the broader industry rather than representing AI reshaping the cognitive or knowledge work of skilled welders. AI might assist peripherally with blueprint interpretation or quality inspection, but the core job remains physical and resistant to AI displacement.
education_detail: Employers often prefer or require candidates to have a high school diploma or equivalent and technical training. This training may be available through high school technical education classes or programs at vocational–technical institutes, community colleges, and private welding, soldering, and brazing schools. In addition, the U.S. Armed Forces offer welding-related training. Courses in blueprint reading, shop mathematics, and mechanical drawing may be helpful. An understanding of electricity also is useful. Workers also may enter the occupation through an employer-based apprenticeship program. Some apprenticeships are available for entry-level workers who have no prior experience or training, while others are targeted toward those who have completed a vocational–technical school welding program. Although some employers hire inexperienced entry-level workers and train them on the job, many prefer to hire workers who have completed training or credentialing programs. Entry-level workers with formal technical training still receive several months of on-the-job training.
certifications: Welders must be licensed in some states and localities; requirements vary. Contact individual state or local government licensing agencies for more information. Professional organizations offer courses leading to general certification. Some employers require general or specific certification for particular jobs. They may pay the cost of training and testing for employees. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. TheOccupational Safety and Health Administration(OSHA) requires that welders complete training on electrical safety. Other types of OSHA training are available but generally are not required.

title: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives
exposure_rationale: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives blend significant interpersonal, relationship-driven work (client visits, negotiations, trust-building) with digital tasks like order processing, reporting, and communication that AI can readily assist with or automate. AI tools such as CRM automation, chatbots, and lead-generation platforms are already encroaching on inside sales functions, while outside sales roles retain a human advantage in complex negotiations and relationship management. The BLS itself notes that AI and automation may limit employment growth, but the physical travel, face-to-face selling, and nuanced client relationship aspects provide a meaningful buffer against full AI displacement.

ai_paths.reason: Managing vendor relationships and coordinating complex product delivery schedules.
Optimizing inventory turnover and maintaining high-volume supplier relationships.
Deep knowledge of logistics, distribution routes, and product handling.
Direct application of persuasion, product knowledge, and closing techniques.
Using interpersonal communication to advocate for and educate clients.
Applying manufacturing standards knowledge to ensure product specification compliance.

education_detail: A high school diploma is typically sufficient for many positions, primarily those selling nontechnical or nonscientific products. However, representatives selling scientific and technical products usually must have a bachelor’s degree. Scientific and technical products include pharmaceuticals, medical instruments, and industrial equipment. Afield of degreerelated to the product sold, such asagricultureorbiology, is sometimes required. Many sales representatives attend seminars in sales techniques or take courses in marketing, economics, communication, or even a foreign language to improve their ability to make sales.
certifications: Although not required, some wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives choose to earn professional certification. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop.

title: Wind turbine technicians
exposure_rationale: Wind turbine technicians perform predominantly physical, hands-on work — climbing 200-foot towers, replacing mechanical components, lubricating parts, and rappelling to service blades — all of which require physical presence and cannot be done remotely by AI. AI might assist with remote monitoring, diagnostics, and maintenance scheduling, but the core job requires a human body in a physically demanding and often hazardous environment. The digital/analytical components (using computers to diagnose electrical malfunctions, collecting turbine data) are a minor fraction of the overall role.
education_detail: Windtechs typically attend technical schools or community colleges, where they may complete a postsecondary certificate in wind energy technology or choose to earn an associate’s degree. Many technical schools have onsite wind turbines that students service as part of their studies. In addition to hands-on learning, windtech coursework includes maintenance instruction for electrical and hydraulic systems, braking and mechanical systems, and programmable logic control systems. Students also receive instruction in tower climbing, along with training for rescues, safety, first aid, and CPR.
certifications: Although not mandatory, professional certification allows workers to demonstrate a certain level of knowledge and competence. Certification subjects for windtechs include workplace electrical safety, tower climbing, and self-rescue. Employers often direct workers to the certifications they need.

industries.name: Power and communication line and related structures construction
Commercial and industrial machinery and equipment (except automotive and electronic) repair and maintenance
Machinery, equipment, and supplies merchant wholesalers

title: Woodworkers
exposure_rationale: Woodworking is fundamentally a physical, hands-on occupation requiring manual dexterity, physical strength, and direct interaction with materials and machinery. While CNC machines and computer-aided design already automate some aspects of the work, the core tasks — cutting, shaping, assembling, finishing, and installing wood products — require physical presence and skilled manual labor that AI cannot directly replace. AI may marginally assist with design interpretation or machine programming, but the occupation's primary barriers to automation are physical, not informational.
education_detail: A high school diploma is typically required to enter the occupation. Training in computer applications and math may enhance employment prospects. For woodworking production jobs, employers may prefer to hire candidates who have taken some vocational-technical or college courses.
certifications: Although not required, credentials often demonstrate competence and professionalism. They also may help a candidate advance in the occupation. Information on certifications is available fromCareerOneStop. Because of the prevalence of CNC machines in production, workers also may benefit from obtaining CNC machine certification. Certification is offered by community colleges and CNC machine manufacturers.

title: Writers and authors
exposure_rationale: Writing and authorship is almost entirely a digital, knowledge-based occupation where the core work product — text — is precisely what modern AI systems excel at generating, editing, and refining. AI tools like large language models can already draft articles, blog posts, ad copy, scripts, and other content at scale, directly threatening the volume of work available to human writers. While originality, deep subject expertise, unique voice, and human relationships with editors/clients provide some buffer, the fundamental task of producing written content is highly automatable, and many lower-tier writing jobs (copywriting, content writing, blogging) are already being displaced.

ai_paths.reason: Storytelling, narrative structure, and creative vision transfer directly.
Creating educational content and explaining complex ideas clearly.
Critical analysis, neutral perspective, and precise language usage.
Persuasive writing and compelling storytelling to engage donors.
Subject matter expertise and ability to communicate concepts.
Managing deadlines, research, and complex document workflows.
Researching history and crafting narratives for public exhibits.
Deep knowledge of literature, composition, and communication skills.

education_detail: Writers and authors typically need a bachelor'sdegreeinEnglishor a related field, such ascommunicationsor journalism.

industries.name: Educational services; state, local, and private
Religious, grantmaking, civic, professional, and similar organizations

title: Zoologists and wildlife biologists
exposure_rationale: Zoologists and wildlife biologists split their time between physical fieldwork (collecting specimens, observing animals in remote habitats, handling wildlife) and knowledge work (data analysis, writing reports, GIS modeling, conservation planning). AI can meaningfully assist with the analytical and writing components — species identification from images, population modeling, literature synthesis — but the core fieldwork requiring physical presence in unpredictable natural environments creates a substantial barrier to AI displacement. The occupation's heavy reliance on outdoor skills, direct animal observation, and hands-on data collection keeps overall AI exposure moderate.
education_detail: Zoologists and wildlife biologists typically need a bachelor’s degree to enter the occupation. Students may pursue a degree in zoology, wildlife biology, or a relatedfield, such asnatural resources. Some students major inbiologyand take coursework in zoology and wildlife biology. Zoologists and wildlife biologists typically need at least a master’s degree for higher level positions and a Ph.D. for independent research positions. Coursework in undergraduate and graduate-level science programs often includes academic, laboratory, and field work. In addition, students may need to take mathematics and statistics to learn data analysis. Zoology and wildlife biology students may gain practical experience through internships, volunteer work, or other employment during college.
